title: Handshake escrow and client-abort semantics for streaming sessions (OpenAI compatibility)
preview: Motivation: intermittent client disconnects during long-running streaming responses cause two classes of problems: 1) clients that reconnect within a short window are unable to resume because the server immediately tears down session state; 2) client-initiated cancels (or network-triggered resets) have ambiguous semantics with respect to tool/function call idempotency and structured output flushing. This PR introduces a handshake escrow token and clarified client-abort semantics to make resume/reconnect and abort behavior deterministic and OpenAI-compatible. Key changes: - Introduce a short-lived session escrow ticket (Escrow-Token) retained server-side for a configurable grace window to support reconnect-and-resume for active stream sessions. The token is returned in initial stream response headers and via the OpenAI-compat protocol extension. - Define deterministic semantics for client-initiated aborts: a cancelling client sends a terminal CANCEL event; server replies with a 499-equivalent closure in the OpenAI adapter and marks the session state as aborted but keeps an abort-marker for the escrow window to allow idempotent retries for in-flight tool calls. - On reconnect-with-ticket, runtime replays the last stable checkpointed structured-output frame (if any) and resumes token emission; if the abort-marker is present the reconnect is rejected unless the reconnect explicitly requests idempotent-retry scope. - Add explicit flush semantics for structured outputs and tool-call boundaries: runtime now ensures a terminal JSON frame is emitted and persisted before freeing durable KV for that session. This reduces split-output and truncation cases seen in production. - Compatibility mapping: map client cancel to HTTP/499 in our OpenAI adapter and document the header-based Escrow-Token workflow for clients migrating from the legacy resume token. - Safety: by default escrow window is 30s, configurable per-tenant; escrow tickets are single-use and rotated on successful resume. - Tests and docs: unit tests for handshake lifecycle, integration streaming smoke tests for reconnect/resume, updated API docs and SDK examples (Python/TS) showing Escrow-Token usage and abort/retry patterns. The description below includes a short migration checklist and operational knobs.

title: add content-disposition/metadata guards and per-tool payload quotas for multipart uploads
preview: Motivation: Tool/file attachments used by the OpenAI-compatible tool-calling surface were allowing poorly formed multipart uploads and oversized payloads which caused streaming stalls and OOMs in the serving runtime. This PR introduces explicit guards around Content-Disposition and file metadata, per-tool and per-account payload quotas for multipart/form-data parts, improved multipart boundary handling to avoid partial-read hangs in stream mode, and small compatibility tweaks so the OpenAI compatibility layer surfaces a stable `files` metadata object for tool calls. Changes are intentionally conservative: they add validation and soft-failure modes (400 + diagnostics) by default and an env flag to opt into hard rejection for stricter enterprise deployments. The description below summarizes goals, changes, test coverage, and rollout plan.

title: Hybrid comm/compute: staggered NCCL streams + dynamic kernel placement for tensor-parallel workloads
preview:
Motivation: For TP models we observed tail latency spikes when large collective operations serialized across the default NCCL stream and blocked compute kernels on subsequent micro-batches. This PR implements a hybrid comm/compute execution strategy: staggered NCCL comm streams per tensor-parallel group, opportunistic kernel placement on secondary compute streams, and a lightweight dynamic placement heuristic that routes small-kernel work to a dedicated low-latency stream to maximize overlap without increasing memory pressure.

Summary of changes: implemented a per-TP-group stream allocator that staggers all-reduce/all-gather launches across NCCL streams (comm_stream_0..N) to reduce contention; added a kernel placement layer in the runtime scheduler that classifies kernels by inferred latency (fast vs slow) and assigns them to either the main compute stream or a fast-path microbatch stream; introduced a KV-cache prefetch gate that issues early prefetches on a priority stream when sequence prefix hits are predicted; added guards for CUDA Graph capture compatibility and a deterministic fallback for constrained environments.

Design notes and rationale: staggering is conservative by default (stride = 2) and can be tuned per-deployment via runtime config (SERVER_TP_COMM_STRIDE). The placement heuristic uses a small sampled histogram of kernel durations (collected during warmup) and a tunable threshold; thresholds are persisted in the worker cache and aged out after config changes. We avoid aggressive fusion for very large kernels because it increases register pressure and can reduce overall throughput on A100-like hardware. The KV prefetch gate reduces latency for long context prefix hits by pre-warming the attention KV buffers on the priority stream while the previous micro-batch tokens finish compute on the main stream.

Testing and benchmarks: microbench harness updated under perf/bench/tp to include a staggered-comm profile. Results on a 16xA100-80GB cluster (tensor-parallel 8 across 2 GPUs per node) show: 9-14% single-request p99 latency reduction for 1-4 token continuations, and 8-12% end-to-end throughput improvement for large-batch streaming workloads when using the default stride=2. Memory overhead observed ~1.7% due to additional stream state and a small histogram buffer. Bench scripts and raw data attached in the PR (perf/bench/results/pr-24672/).

Files touched: runtime scheduler, low-level NCCL wrapper, kernel metadata table, kvcache prefetch codepath, perf harness and tests, docs note in docs/perf/tp-comm-tuning.md.

Migration and rollout: feature guarded behind runtime flag tp.hybrid_comm.enabled=false by default on main. Canary rollout plan: enable on dedicated performance canaries (redwood/perf-canary) for 72h, monitor token-level latency traces and p99 regressions. Automatic rollback if p99 increases by >3% or if GPU OOMs are observed.

Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for stream allocator and placement heuristic - [x] Integration test (simulated multi-GPU TP in CI) - [x] Perf harness and baseline comparison - [x] Docs and runtime config entries - [x] Canary/rollback plan

Related work: builds on prior NCCL tuning efforts (see ENG-1684 and prior PRs) but differs by introducing dynamic kernel placement and KV prefetch gating. Addresses customer case SUP-455 that reported tail latency on TP configs.

Notes: kept the changes small and feature-flagged to reduce blast radius. This PR intentionally does not enable CUDA Graph capture when the hybrid comm path is active; that follow-up will require graph replay orchestration and is tracked as ENG-1899.

title: compat: strict media negotiation + per-file ingestion-rate-limits for tool attachments
preview:
Context: Our OpenAI-compat layer has had recurring issues when clients upload ambiguous or binary attachments without clear content-type or length headers. These cause parsing retries, confused streaming boundaries, and occasional function_call failures when tool runtimes receive corrupted payloads. This PR introduces two complementary protections: (1) deterministic media negotiation and normalization so the compat layer always annotates attachments with a canonical content-type + checksum; (2) per-file ingestion rate limits and adaptive chunk checks to prevent slow/bursty clients from overwhelming streaming parsers and downstream tool runtimes.

What this changes (high level):
- Compat layer now enforces an Accept/Content-Type negotiation step for incoming attachments. Preferred content-types for tool calls are application/json (pointer/meta), application/octet-stream (binary payloads), and multipart/form-data for multi-part uploads. Ambiguous cases (e.g. text/plain without charset for likely binary data) are rejected with a 400 and a clear error that lists accepted types.
- Attachments without a declared content-type will trigger a lightweight sniff: if first 512 bytes look like UTF-8 JSON/text we label application/json or text/plain; otherwise we label application/octet-stream. Sniffing is deterministic and logged for audits.
- Each file stream now carries an enforced per-file ingress rate limit (configurable per-customer and per-route). By default set to 4 MB/s for hosted API and 8 MB/s for Dedicated. Exceeding the rate returns a 429 with Retry-After and a hint header (X-File-Rate-Limit).
- For streaming uploads we added a small windowing protocol: the compat proxy tolerates out-of-order chunk arrival within a 500ms reorder window, but rejects incremental chunks that lack proper boundary markers when rate limits are tripped. This significantly reduces repeated partial-parsing in the serving runtime.
- For function/tool calls that reference files, we now require file metadata (content_type, size, sha256) in the function_call arguments. If missing, the compat layer computes sha256 and injects metadata before handing to the tool runtime. This prevents tool runtimes from having to re-read streams and reduces platform I/O.

Why this is safe and backward compatible:
- Well-formed clients that already send content-type and standard multipart/form-data will be unaffected.
- Clients that relied on opaque coercion of text/plain->binary will now get a clear error and guidance to set Content-Type or wrap the file as base64 in JSON. The error message includes a one-line migration hint to keep developer friction low.

Tests & validation included:
- Unit tests for negotiation logic, sniff heuristics, and sha256 injection.
- Integration tests that exercise multipart uploads, chunked transfer-encoding streams, and slow-client scenarios using our perf-canary harness.
- End-to-end tests: function_call with file refs across redwood (compat) -> runtime -> tool-invoker. Added a compatibility matrix test that runs against a matrix of SDK versions (python:1.x/2.x, ts:1.x) to ensure we don't regress client expectations.

Checklist: 
- [x] Implement negotiation + sniffing code in compat proxy
- [x] Add per-file rate-limiter middleware with config knobs on route/customer level
- [x] Compute and inject file metadata (sha256, content_type) for function_call refs
- [x] Add logging + audit events for sniffing decisions
- [x] Add unit/integration/e2e tests
- [x] Update docs/compat-guide (short migration blurb)

Related: ENG-2330 (rate limiting webhooks influenced design), PM-411 (docs/migration text), SUP-981 (customer incident that motivated stricter errors).

title: v1/chat: stabilize streaming handshake; enforce stop-token precedence and introduce role-alias mapping
preview: Context: Customers integrating via the OpenAI /v1/chat.completions surface have seen two classes of issues in production: 1) transient stream disconnects during tool/function call handoffs (client resumes see inconsistent framing), and 2) ambiguity in how stop tokens are applied when stop arrays are provided alongside role-level stop hints or tool-generated token sequences. This PR unifies several compatibility behaviors to reduce surprise for downstream clients and to harden streaming handshakes during function/tool call routing. Motivation: Provide predictable, backward-compatible semantics for v1 chat parity while reducing the failure surface during streaming toolcalls. Goals: (a) stabilize the streaming handshake so that tool/function call initiation and the subsequent resumption of the assistant stream never produce overlapping or dropped deltas, (b) define and implement a deterministic stop-token precedence order (explicit request param > tool-produced stop sequence > role-level hints), (c) add an opt-in role_alias mapping so customers sending alternative role names (e.g., "assistant_bot", "system_agent") map to canonical OpenAI roles for compatibility, and (d) wrap logprobs and structured scores in a deterministic envelope that matches OpenAI parity expectations when streaming deltas are emitted. Changes: - runtime/stream: implement a two-phase handshake for toolcall handoff with explicit ACK/FIN markers and idempotent resume tokens so retries do not duplicate tokens - api/openai_compat: introduce a stop precedence resolver that accepts stop arrays and role/tool hints and returns the canonical stop set applied to generation - api/openai_compat: add role_alias config parsing and mapping; default mappings included and overridable via server config - models/compat: attach a small envelope to streaming delta messages: choices[].delta.{content,role,tool_call,logprobs} to avoid ambiguous flattened fields - sdk/python: update OpenAI-compat adapter to accept role_alias and expose resolved_stop in response metadata - tests: new integration tests exercising stream resume, stop precedence and role aliasing Backwards compatibility and rollout: default behavior is backward-compatible; the stop precedence policy is additive and only changes behavior when multiple stop sources are present (rare). Role-alias mapping is opt-in per-tenant via configuration flag; we set sane defaults for common aliases. The streaming handshake change is released behind a runtime feature-flag; feature is enabled for canary tenants and then rolled out to all after 72h of telemetry. Checklist: [x] unit tests for precedence resolver [x] integration test for stream resume [x] SDK adapter updates [x] e2e smoke in Canary [x] docs note in openai/v1_chat.md Testing: CI includes unit and integration runs. Canary telemetry evaluated for token duplication and error rates for 72h. Observability: added new metrics stream.handshake_retries, stream.duplicate_delta_count, compat.role_alias_mappings_applied Related: ENG-1542 (stream stability), ENG-1588 (openai compat stop semantics), SUP-4321 customer report Refs: docs/openai/v1_chat.md, runtime/stream/README.md

title: Optimize INT8 dequant-fusion kernel and smoothquant scheduling to reduce p99 and token cost
preview:
Motivation: For the quantized inference stack we've been shipping two main bottlenecks: (1) the dequantization+matmul fusion path for INT8 kernels introduces extra memory traffic and kernel launches at each attention and FFN block; (2) SmoothQuant scaling interacts poorly with our scheduler and continuous-batching heuristics, causing variable p99 regressions under mixed-sequence workloads. This PR introduces three coordinated changes: 1) a fused dequantize+gemm kernel that accepts packed INT8 weights and produces FP16 accumulation with optional requantization hooks; 2) a scheduling tweak that biases smoothquant scaling application to the slower stage of the pipeline and batches compatible sequence shapes together more aggressively; 3) a lightweight KV-cache-aware fast path that skips per-step dequant for cached KV blocks and reuses FP16 cached keys when the model quantization profile allows it.
High-level summary of changes: - Implement fused_int8_dequant_gemm CUDA kernel with vectorized loads, int8->fp16 accumulation, and single-kernel scale+bias application; exposed via runtime/kernel_registry. - Add quant_profile metadata to model descriptors to select the optimal kernel variant at load time (INT8_PACKED, FP8_DYNAMIC, FP16_FALLBACK). - Scheduler: introduce SequenceShapeAffinity rule to group similar-length requests for continuous batching; add SmoothQuantWindow heuristic to only apply per-layer scaling on first N tokens of a request when streaming is enabled. - KV cache: store an optional FP16 shadow of int8-packed KV blocks for reuse; add LRU soft cap and transparent eviction. - Telemetry: new perf counters (kernel_time_fused_int8, kv_shadow_hit, smoothquant_applied) and unit tests.
Performance numbers (microbench on T4 and A10G; averaged across 50k requests of mixed lengths 16/128/512): - Baseline (old INT8 path): p50 12.8ms p95 38.2ms p99 78.5ms throughput 22 toks/s - This PR (fused + scheduling + kv-shadow): p50 11.4ms p95 29.6ms p99 49.3ms throughput 28 toks/s - Relative: p99 reduced ~37%, throughput +27%, token GPU time reduced ~18%.
Safety and compatibility: default model load remains unchanged; INT8 packed models must declare quant_profile or fallback to old path. This avoids accidental perf regressions for older deployments.
Checklist: - [x] Kernel implementation + unit tests - [x] Scheduler heuristics and e2e perf harness - [x] KV-shadow LRU + eviction tests - [x] Telemetry and console dashboard metric wiring - [x] Backwards-compatible model descriptor changes - [x] Integration smoke runs on Dedicated cluster
Implementation notes: commits include a sequence of focused patches (see commits field). The fused kernel uses 128-bit vector loads and tail-handling similar to other fused kernels in kernels/cuda/; we add a small inline assembly path for faster requant when supported by arch. The scheduler change is additive: SequenceShapeAffinity is a soft hint; it will not prevent immediate dispatch when latency SLO is tight. KV-shadow stores fp16 only when the quant_profile allows dequant-lossless shadowing.
Files changed (high-level): - runtime/kernel_registry.py: register fused_int8_dequant_gemm - kernels/cuda/fused_int8_dequant_gemm.cu: new kernel implementation and tests - runtime/scheduler/affinity.py: SequenceShapeAffinity and SmoothQuantWindow heuristics - runtime/kv_cache/kv_shadow.py: FP16 shadow store, eviction policy - model/quant_profiles.py: quant_profile schema and loaders - telemetry/metrics.py: new counters and tagging - tests/perf/test_quant_kernels.py: microbench harness and assertions - docs/quantization.md: updated guidance and model packaging notes
Commits (summary): - a1c3f8a: initial fused kernel prototype and unit tests - b4d2e10: vectorization, tail handling, and arch-specific paths - c7f9b01: expose kernel via kernel_registry and add quant_profile selector - d2a8f44: implement SequenceShapeAffinity scheduler rule and tests - e5b1c3f: KV-shadow store with LRU and eviction unit tests - f1039d7: telemetry counters and console dashboard wires - f8b6a22: perf tuning, CI fixes, and documentation
Update/Review conversation (selected): - Priya Natarajan (reviewer): "Great direction. My main concern is memory overhead for the FP16 KV shadow under high-concurrency. Can we bound memory and prioritize eviction for older sequences?" Author reply (Ethan): "Answered: KV-shadow uses an LRU soft cap with eviction triggered at 0.6*mem_limit and immediate reclamation to 0.8*mem_limit to avoid thrashing; added bench in tests to ensure p99 doesn't regress under pressure." - Liam O'Connor: "Kernel looks good; please add more comments around the arch-specific asm path and a fallback when not supported." Author: "Added comments and a runtime is_x86_avx512 check; fallback to scalar path included." - Sara Kim: "How does this interact with canary rollout? We need a safe toggle in runtime to opt-out quickly." Author: "Added runtime feature flag runtime.flags.quant_fused_enabled and a per-deployment override exposed to Dedicated/Private configs; tests added."
CI checks summary: - linux-build: pass - unit-tests: pass - perf-regression: pass (bench run) - flake8/lint: pass - integration-smoke(dedicated): pass
Merge outcome: merged via squash on 2026-03-05 by Ethan Zhao; merge commit f9c2d11. Post-merge actions: - Backfill quant_profile to community model registry (docs/quantization.md has migration steps) - Dashboard alert to watch kernel_time_fused_int8 p99 for next 72 hours - Coordinate with Customer Success for a small set of Dedicated customers to enable feature flag during next maintenance window.

High-level summary of changes: - Implement fused_int8_dequant_gemm CUDA kernel with vectorized loads, int8->fp16 accumulation, and single-kernel scale+bias application; exposed via runtime/kernel_registry. - Add quant_profile metadata to model descriptors to select the optimal kernel variant at load time (INT8_PACKED, FP8_DYNAMIC, FP16_FALLBACK). - Scheduler: introduce SequenceShapeAffinity rule to group similar-length requests for continuous batching; add SmoothQuantWindow heuristic to only apply per-layer scaling on first N tokens of a request when streaming is enabled. - KV cache: store an optional FP16 shadow of int8-packed KV blocks for reuse; add LRU soft cap and transparent eviction. - Telemetry: new perf counters (kernel_time_fused_int8, kv_shadow_hit, smoothquant_applied) and unit tests.
Performance numbers (microbench on T4 and A10G; averaged across 50k requests of mixed lengths 16/128/512): - Baseline (old INT8 path): p50 12.8ms p95 38.2ms p99 78.5ms throughput 22 toks/s - This PR (fused + scheduling + kv-shadow): p50 11.4ms p95 29.6ms p99 49.3ms throughput 28 toks/s - Relative: p99 reduced ~37%, throughput +27%, token GPU time reduced ~18%.
Safety and compatibility: default model load remains unchanged; INT8 packed models must declare quant_profile or fallback to old path. This avoids accidental perf regressions for older deployments.
Checklist: - [x] Kernel implementation + unit tests - [x] Scheduler heuristics and e2e perf harness - [x] KV-shadow LRU + eviction tests - [x] Telemetry and console dashboard metric wiring - [x] Backwards-compatible model descriptor changes - [x] Integration smoke runs on Dedicated cluster
Implementation notes: commits include a sequence of focused patches (see commits field). The fused kernel uses 128-bit vector loads and tail-handling similar to other fused kernels in kernels/cuda/; we add a small inline assembly path for faster requant when supported by arch. The scheduler change is additive: SequenceShapeAffinity is a soft hint; it will not prevent immediate dispatch when latency SLO is tight. KV-shadow stores fp16 only when the quant_profile allows dequant-lossless shadowing.

Can we bound memory and prioritize eviction for older sequences?" Author reply (Ethan): "Answered: KV-shadow uses an LRU soft cap with eviction triggered at 0.6*mem_limit and immediate reclamation to 0.8*mem_limit to avoid thrashing; added bench in tests to ensure p99 doesn't regress under pressure." - Liam O'Connor: "Kernel looks good; please add more comments around the arch-specific asm path and a fallback when not supported." We need a safe toggle in runtime to opt-out quickly." title: hybrid-staged-canary-controller-with-statistical-gates-and-fallthrough-routing
preview: Motivation: enable safer multi-stage experiments and automated rollback by introducing a hybrid staged canary controller that combines time/traffic phasing, Bayesian statistical gates, and deterministic fallthrough routing. This PR implements a controller that can run A/B tests, shadow trials, and progressive canaries using a configurable stability window, significance threshold, and quarantine queue. Key features: 1) BayesianSignificanceEngine (configurable priors) to decide pass/fail for metric families; 2) HealthSnapshot store that captures per-route histograms and model KV cache hit rates at sample intervals; 3) WeightDecaySchedule to gradually reduce canary traffic on persistent regressions before triggering full rollback; 4) FallthroughRouting that routes traffic to a fallback model variant instantly on hard failures while preserving request affinity; 5) QuarantineQueue and automated re-evaluation that isolates problematic model variants and automatically schedules a re-test after a cool-down with adjusted batch/quant settings; 6) DriftDetector to abort experiments when input distribution divergence exceeds threshold; 7) Integration hooks for Redwood Optimize to emit suggested quantization/batching profiles for canaries. Checklist: - [x] controller implementation and reconciliation loop - [x] Bayesian engine + unit tests - [x] health snapshot persistence and API - [x] fallthrough routing and affinity preservation - [x] quarantine queue and automated re-test job - [x] shadow traffic passthrough mode - [x] docs and runbook updates - [x] e2e smoke test in perf-canary harness How to test locally: run ./hack/dev-canary with two local model variants, set linear key ENG-9123 as test target, verify that induced regression (synthetic label noise) triggers weight decay then quarantine and eventual rollback. Backward compatibility: defaults preserve previous canary semantics if hybrid_mode=false. Migration: adds CRD fields canary.strategy and canary.statsEngine but controller tolerates absent fields. Related: ENG-9123 for rollout design, ENG-9188 for metric extraction, SUP-447 incident notes. Impact: improves safety and reduces noisy manual rollbacks; expected to reduce manual interventions by ~40% for staged rollouts (internal estimate).

title: fragmented-kernel-workspace-pool-kv-rolling-reserve-and-adaptive-batching-prioritizer
preview: Motivation: Under heavy continuous-batching workloads we observe fragmented temporary workspace allocations and conservative KV cache reservations that inflate peak GPU memory by ~15-25%. This PR introduces a kernel-fragment pool allocator, a compacting fragment coalescer, a KV rolling-reserve policy with age-based demotion, and an adaptive batching prioritizer that protects tail latency while increasing packing efficiency. Goals: 1) reduce peak transient workspace (scratch) footprint, 2) lower KV cache reserved overhead for workloads with skewed hotsets, 3) keep 95p/99p latency equal or improved under steady-state continuous batching. Changes (high level): - runtime/worker: implement FragmentPool allocator that suballocates kernel workspace into variable-length fragments mapped to kernel affinity classes; supports fast reuse and best-fit coalescing on free. - runtime/scheduler: add AdaptiveBatchPrioritizer which reorders queued requests to reduce internal fragmentation and avoid long-lived large-activation requests from blocking smaller quick requests (tail-latency guardrails). - runtime/kvcache: KV rolling-reserve: reserve window size per-model by observed hit-age histogram; demote rarely accessed entries from reserved to spillable on a time-decay basis. - runtime/alloc: optional lightweight compaction pass that coalesces adjacent free fragments during low-load periods. - tests & tooling: new microbench harnesses for fragmented allocation patterns, end-to-end perf scenario files, and flamegraph capture hooks for workspace churn. Implementation notes: - FragmentPool is lock-free per-GPU-stream for fast path allocation; fallback to pooled mutex for rare large allocations. - Coalescing is limited to a short-duration cooperative pass triggered by low utilization to avoid impacting latency. - Rolling-reserve tracks per-key last-access epoch and uses a small LRU-friendly structure to decide demotion; no synchronous CPU writeback in hot path. Performance summary (measured on 8xA100, config: steady continuous batching, mixed length): - Peak GPU memory usage: -18% median, -23% under the 95th percentile scenarios. - 95p latency: -12% (reduction) for short requests; unchanged for long requests. - Throughput (tokens/sec): +6% due to improved packing and reduced stalls. Safety and fallback: All features are disabled by default for existing customers; new flags are behind runtime flags and can be toggled per-deployment. Rolling-reserve defaults to conservative limits; fragmentation coalescer disabled on single-GPU hosts. Checklist: - [x] unit tests for FragmentPool api - [x] integration tests for scheduler reordering - [x] KV reserve regression tests - [x] perf harness runs (attached) - [x] docs entry for runtime flags Migration/compatibility: No API changes; runtime flags control behavior. Observability: added metrics: fragpool.alloc_count, fragpool.coalesce_time_ms, kv.reserve_demotions, batch.prioritizer_reorders. Related: ENG-5643 (workspaces), ENG-5711 (KV hotset sizing).

title: tokenized-fairness-scaler-and-util-gap-reporter
preview:
Context and motivation: customers on Dedicated have asked for better fairness when bursts co-exist with steady throughput tenants. Existing burst pools and reservations ensure throughput but can starve latency-sensitive tenants during microbursts. This PR implements a tokenized fairness scaler: a lightweight token accounting layer that mints per-tenant 'burst tokens' proportional to committed throughput and dynamically redistributes spare tokens based on queue-latency buckets.

Design summary: the scaler observes per-tenant request queue latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99) and token consumption rate. When a tenant's p95 exceeds its latency target and tokens are available in the cluster pool, the allocator grants ephemeral tokens that raise the tenant's concurrency cap for short windows (configurable TTL). Tokens are reclaimed proactively when latency recovers or when they expire. Token minting respects tenant share weights and a soft floor so smaller tenants cannot be starved.

Utilization Gap Reporter: a new telemetry pipeline sampler that computes 'util-gap' = committed_capacity - observed_effective_throughput over rolling windows. Significant positive gap (>X%) indicates underutilized reserved capacity; negative gap suggests chronic oversubscription and triggers alerts. The reporter aggregates per-tenant and per-cluster signals and emits events to the Console and to the autoscaler's feedback loop.

Key behaviors and safety: - Autoscaler only grants tokens if cluster headroom >= headroom_threshold config. - Tokens are time-limited (default 30s) and double-checked before increasing concurrency to avoid drift. - Manual overrides: operators can pin token budgets per-tenant via the Dedicated Console. - Feature gated: flag 'tokenized_fair_scaler_v1' controls rollout.

Files of note: see changed files list below for implementation and tests.

Testing and validation: - Unit tests for token accounting, TTL expiry, and reclaim logic. - Integration tests with synthetic traffic generator that simulates bursty and steady tenants and validates latency SLOs improve without increasing aggregate CPU/Tensor utilization. - Canary plan: deploy to a single Dedicated cluster with low-risk tenants (internal sandbox), monitor util-gap and latency deltas for 48h, then expand to two clusters before full rollout.

Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked (ENG-1842 tokenized-fairness-scaler) - [x] Unit & integration tests - [x] Telemetry for util-gap metrics - [x] Console toggles and tenant budget API - [x] Feature flag behind canary - [x] Safety tests and chaos run

Related: references existing autoscaler work (ENG-1532) but intentionally keeps runtime changes minimal to reduce risk.

title: per-layer kernel occupancy probes + adaptive batch-window tracer (KV-stamp profiler)
preview:
Summary: Instrumentation and microbench additions to help diagnose layer-level kernel occupancy and batch-window interactions with KV-cache. This PR introduces a lightweight per-layer occupancy probe, a stamp-based KV-cache sampler ("kv-stamp profiler"), and an adaptive batch-window tracer that records contiguous request grouping behavior used by the continuous-batching scheduler. All probes are zero-cost when disabled and are guarded behind runtime flags and RBAC-controlled debug endpoints.

Motivation and context: We observed tail-latency regressions under mixed request shapes where a few large layers dominated GPU occupancy and caused scheduler-induced head-of-line blocking. Existing coarse counters were insufficient to map slowdowns to individual kernel launches or to correlate KV-cache reuse with batch-window size. This work aims to give engineers the tools to: (1) attribute occupancy regressions to specific model layers/kernels, (2) measure the effective batch-window (number of requests grouped per dispatch) over time, and (3) correlate KV-cache hit patterns with per-layer hot paths.

What changed (high level):
- runtime/probes/occupancy_probe.h/.cc: per-layer occupancy probe that hooks into kernel dispatch points and records occupancy (SM utilization estimate) + launch duration. Exports a compact proto-friendly record for tracing.
- runtime/probes/kv_stamp_profiler.h/.cc: stamp-based KV-cache sampler that attaches a short-lived token to requests and samples cache hits/misses across layers to compute reuse windows.
- runtime/scheduler/batch_window_tracer.cc: adaptive tracer that logs when the scheduler decides to flush a batch, capturing batch composition, sequence lengths, and KV stamps.
- tools/microbench/occupancy_benchdriver: small microbench harness to replay recorded traces at configurable QPS and report distribution of occupancy and batch-window sizes.
- observability/dashboards/occupancy-dashboard.json: Grafana panel definitions and Query Language (PromQL/SQL) mappings to visualize per-layer occupancy heatmap and KV reuse curves.
- controlplane: added debug endpoint /debug/probes to enable/disable probes and stream sampler snapshots (RBAC-protected).

Commits (summary):
- 9f3d2a8: add occupancy probe skeleton and proto definitions
- 1b4a7c2: implement kernel hookpoints and SM-util estimators
- 6c8ef91: add KV-stamp sampling and tag propagation through request context
- d4a11b3: integrate batch-window tracer into continuous batching scheduler
- aebb2f0: microbench harness + runner + synthetic workload generator
- be3c442: dashboards and CI runbook instructions
- c7f0d9a: tests, docs, and RBAC debug endpoint

Files changed (select highlights):
- runtime/probes/occupancy_probe.h (new)
- runtime/probes/occupancy_probe.cc (new)
- runtime/probes/kv_stamp_profiler.h (new)
- runtime/probes/kv_stamp_profiler.cc (new)
- runtime/scheduler/batch_window_tracer.cc (modified)
- tools/microbench/occupancy_benchdriver/main.cc (new)
- observability/dashboards/occupancy-dashboard.json (new)
- controlplane/http/debug_handlers.cc (modified)
- docs/observability/occupancy.md (new)

Testing and results: Unit and integration tests were added for probe lifecycle and stamp propagation. Microbench harness reproduces a regression scenario: mixed traffic with occasional long sequences. Before instrumentation + adaptive tracer, under synthetic 500 qps workload with 5% long requests we observed 99th latency = 312 ms and visible spike in SM contention. After adapting scheduler thresholds (guided by the tracer insights) and enabling KV-stamp-aware coalescing, end-to-end 99th latency dropped to 245 ms (-21%), and average per-layer hotpath occupancy variance reduced by ~34% in the bench runs. Note: these numbers are from the microbench harness on a p4d instance; production effects would vary.

Backward compatibility and safety: All probes are opt-in via the /debug/probes endpoint and a runtime config flag (runtime.probes.enabled=false by default). The probe code avoids allocations on the hot path by using thread-local ring buffers and a lock-free aggregator. The control plane enforces that only users with the observability:admin role can toggle sampling.

Review conversation (selected excerpts):
- Jordan Lee (reviewer): "Nice work — this gives the visibility we needed. A couple of asks: (1) ensure SM-estimator doesn't allocate on every kernel path, (2) add a unit test for stamp propagation across async cuda streams."
- Maya Patel (author): "Addressed both: estimator uses a precomputed LUT and TLS, added stream-propagation test in runtime/probes/tests/kv_stamp_test.cc."
- Priya Natarajan (reviewer): "Grafana panels look good, but let's avoid adding expensive dashboard queries to the default ingest. Recommend materializing a short-term rollup table in the observability pipeline."
- Maya Patel: "Agreed — changed dashboard to use pre-aggregated metrics (probe_occupancy_bucket) and added guidance in docs/observability/occupancy.md."
- Luis Gomez (reviewer & approver): "Approving after small nits. Please document RBAC access in the ops runbook."
- Ops comment (internal): "Temporarily disable probes in canary until we've validated throughput impact at production load."

CI summary: Initial CI run reported flaky unit on runtime/scheduler tests (timing-dependent) which was fixed by introducing a deterministic clock in the test harness. Full CI (build, unit, integration, lint) all passed on final run. Perf check (microbench pipeline) executed on the perf-lab pool; results above included.

Follow-ups and TODOs:
- ENG-3922: Add continuous integration job to run microbench harness nightly against representative instance types.
- ENG-3938: Add end-to-end alerting that surfaces sudden increases in per-layer occupancy variance.
- Investigate tighter kernel-level hooks for future reduction of estimator error under model parallelism.

Merge: Squash-merged into main on 2026-03-05 after approvals. Change is feature-flagged and shipped behind RBAC and runtime flag to minimize risk. The dashboards and docs are included in the same PR to make rollout and troubleshooting immediate.

Related work: complements earlier occupancy counters and tracing exports but focuses on correlating occupancy with KV-cache reuse and continuous-batching behavior. Distinct from prior NVTX/time-trace changes by providing a low-overhead sampled profiler and an adaptive tracer targeted at scheduler decision points.

title: add-inference-health-probe-and-sampled-kernel-queue-explorer
preview: Adds a lightweight inference health probe and a sampled kernel-queue explorer to improve incident triage and long-tail cost attribution. Motivation: recurring short-lived kernel queue stalls were noisy in SLO pages and made it hard to trace whether slow tails were caused by model-level token spikes, kernel contention, or tenant-induced backpressure. This PR introduces: 1) inference_health_probe: periodic synthetic pings that exercise a small, deterministic token path and emit a health metric (inference_probe_latency_ms) plus an opaque probe_id for trace linking; 2) sampled_kernel_queue_explorer: a new trace sampler that, when a probe or real invocation exceeds a kernel-queue latency threshold, captures a compact kernel-queue snapshot and stores a compacted span with queue_depth, head_kernel_id, and estimated_token_cost_hint; 3) dashboard panels: a compact 'Probe Pulse' timeseries, kernel-queue heatmap (bucketed by queue depth and model shard), and a 'Probe vs Real' latency scatter for quick triage; 4) runbook/playbook: an Observability Surge Playbook page with automated escalation steps and quick links to tenant-level cost and KV cache hit panels. Implementation notes: probe logic is opt-in per deployment via config.inference.probe.enabled (false by default) and probes are low-rate by design (0.1 rps per region) to avoid billing noise; the sampled kernel snapshots are stored in the observability compact store with a capped reservoir to limit cardinality. Backwards compatibility: no changes to public API surfaces. Checklist: [x] add probe runtime hooks, [x] add metric and span schema, [x] add dashboard panels, [x] add playbook and runbook links, [x] update docs and config reference, [x] add unit tests and e2e sampler smoke test. Related: ENG-6123 for incident RCA and OBS-47 for dashboard request. Expected rollout: feature gated for 2 weeks, enable for staging then incrementally to prod with guardrail on stored snapshot volume.

title: Graceful disconnect: buffered tool-call retry and reconnect window for streaming API
preview: Motivation: We observed customer incidents where short-lived client disconnects during multi-step function/tool-calling flows caused duplicate tool invocations or orphaned streaming sessions, leading to higher costs and inconsistent structured outputs. This PR introduces a server-side in-flight buffer and a short reconnect window with idempotency reconciliation to preserve end-to-end semantics while keeping latency and cost in check. Summary of changes: 1) Session manager: add a buffered-inflight queue that retains in-progress tool invocations for a configurable reconnect window (default 20s). Sessions now emit a compact session token (reconnect_token) on streaming start. 2) Tool invocation idempotency: introduce X-Tool-Invocation-Id header and runtime-side idempotency table so duplicate replays are deduplicated; tools should return idempotency-safe responses. 3) Reconnect/reconcile flow: upon client reconnect with reconnect_token, server attempts to reconcile buffered inflight work with the client's last-ack position, returning any accumulated partial structured output and the current tool invocation states. If the client reconnects after the window, buffered items are aborted with graceful teardown events. 4) Soft-cancel semantics: new soft-cancel signal allows clients to request an in-flight tool call be aborted only if it has not reached an irreversible side-effect point; otherwise the server completes the call and returns final structured output. 5) SDK/compat: TypeScript and Python SDKs added helper APIs to expose reconnect_token and soft-cancel semantics while preserving OpenAI-compatible streaming events (partial chunks, function_call start/finish events). 6) Observability: new metrics (session.reconnect_attempts, tool.invocation.dedup_hits, session.buffered_items) and traces to aid debugging. 7) Tests: unit tests for reconcile/timeout logic, integration tests simulating flaky client disconnects, and an e2e test that ensures no duplicate tool executions for retries. Checklist: - [x] API design reviewed for OpenAI compatibility - [x] Runtime changes gated behind feature flag streaming_reconnect_v2 - [x] SDK helpers implemented and docstrings added - [x] Metrics and tracing added - [x] Tests included and green on CI Design notes: reconnect_token is short-lived (default TTL 20s) to avoid long-lived server buffering. The buffered queue is size-capped per-session and uses LRU eviction for low-memory impact; overflow causes immediate abort with structured error to client. Soft-cancel is advisory to the runtime and will translate to an AbortableContext inside tool runners; tools can opt-in to fast abort or honor idempotency markers and complete. Compatibility: OpenAI-compatible streaming events are preserved: the server emits a function_call.start event when a tool is invoked, intermittent partial function_call.content chunks during streaming, and function_call.finish when completed; on reconnect the server will stream any missed chunks and then resume new output. CI notes: initial run flagged a flaky e2e test that relied on timing; test was stabilized by switching to event-based waits and re-run. Merge: squash-merged to main on 2026-03-03 after approvals. Impact: reduces duplicate tool calls in flaky network scenarios, gives SDKs a clear reconnect primitive, and improves observability for streaming interruptions. Runtime defaults keep memory/cost bounded and the feature is controlled by feature flags for gradual rollout.

title: Topology-aware peer balancing, async kernel dispatch, and KV-cache coalescing
preview: Motivation: Multi-GPU tensor-parallel workloads suffer from suboptimal collective placement and small-KV churn when requests are micro-batched across heterogeneous racks. This PR implements three coordinated runtime optimizations to improve throughput and tail latency: (1) topology-aware peer balancing: build NVLink/NIC-aware ring/graph membership using a lightweight port-speed probe so collectives prefer intra-node/NVLink lanes and minimize cross-node hops, and dynamically rebalance peer pairs when per-step bandwidth estimates indicate hotlinks; (2) async kernel dispatch: move per-layer kernel launch to a dispatch thread pair that annotates CUDA streams with priority and orders kernels for overlap with NCCL comms, and add a non-blocking enqueue-locality check so compute work can start as soon as required dependencies are queued; (3) KV-cache coalescing at the request router: group concurrent small KV reads into coalesced bulk fetches and fold redundant cache population work to reduce memory traffic and small-launch overheads. Design notes: peer selection runs at model load and is re-evaluated when topology events are seen (PCIe/NVLink health changes, new GPUs added). Peer balancing uses a lightweight EWMA of measured pairwise bandwidths to pick an alternating pairing sequence for each micro-batch window. Async dispatch uses two prioritized CUDA streams per device (compute_high, compute_low) plus a comm stream; we rely on CUDA stream priorities instead of artificial host sleeps which reduces scheduling jitter. KV coalescer is conservatively enabled behind the feature flag tp_topo_pb and has a max-coalesce window (configurable) to avoid added latency for single-request critical paths. Implementation summary: runtime/scheduler: add TopologyProbe and PeerBalancer modules, extend DeviceResources to expose stream priorities; runtime/comm: new TopoRingBuilder that prefers NVLink edges, updates NCCL unique IDs to include pairing hints; runtime/dispatch: KernelDispatcher refactor, introduce DispatchFence for dependency tracking and switch to cudaLaunchKernelAsync for fused kernels; router/kv_cache: RequestCoalescer layer with in-process batching and de-duplication. Compatibility and safety: fallbacks to previous ring builder when topology probe fails or when running on unsupported drivers; coalescer has a conservative timeout and a per-model opt-out. Tests and validation: microbenchmarks using benchmark-lab show 18-25% throughput improvement on 8xA100 NVLink topology for half-precision decode workloads with 2048 seq; 95th percentile tail latency reduced by ~14-20% for 8k token streams when KV coalescing is enabled. Perf canary across 3 dedicated clusters showed stable error rates and no observable degradation in output quality. Rollout plan: feature-flagged in main, staged canary to dedicated-qa, then incremental ramp to production Dedicated pools over 72 hours with monitoring on NCCL error counters, GPU PCIe errors, end-to-end latency SLOs. Observability: emit metrics peer_balance/pair_changes, topo_probe/latency_ms, kvcoalesce/coalesce_rate, dispatcher/stream_stall_ms, and add tracing spans for coalescer windows. Documentation: updated runtime/README and redwood-docs/perf for recommended config knobs. CI and perf runs: included unit tests for peer balancer invariants and integration tests in perf-harness. This change is primarily internal runtime improvements; no user API changes expected.

title: Smoother autoscaler signal and gateway backpressure for dedicated pools
preview:
Summary: Introduces a smoothing layer in the Dedicated Autoscaler and a lightweight backpressure signal propagated from the gateway to fleet controllers. This reduces autoscaler thrash on spiky traffic and gives gateways a controlled way to shed load gracefully against per-pool soft capacity and throughput guarantees.

Motivation: Customers on Dedicated have reported oscillations when short bursts cross autoscaler thresholds and then recede; this leads to unnecessary GPU spin-ups and billing variance, as well as transient error spikes. The gateway currently responds with 429s or fallback heuristics; we need a first-class, observable mechanism that cooperates with autoscaling and preserves headroom for SLA-critical requests.

Changes in this PR:
- Autoscaler: add Exponential Moving Average (EMA) smoothing of utilization and request rate with configurable half-life per-pool. New config fields: smoothing_window_ms, smoothing_alpha (derived).
- Soft capacity & grace tokens: each pool now tracks a small ephemeral token bucket (grace_tokens) to allow short bursts without triggering scale-up, decaying over time and replenished by underutilization.
- Gateway backpressure: add a new X-Redwood-Backpressure header and a compact gRPC/backchannel (gateway->controller) event to advertise desired ingress rate reductions per-route when pool smoothed_utilization > threshold.
- Controller/gateway handshake: gateway subscribes to per-pool backpressure_quota updates; controllers include backpressure_rate in scheduling decisions and expose metrics.
- Telemetry: new metrics smoothed_utilization, grace_tokens_available, backpressure_rate, backpressure_rejections. Add Prometheus labels for pool_id, model_id, and region.
- API schema: extend dedicated pool spec with smoothing and backpressure knobs; added validation and defaults.
- Console/docs: admin UI now surfaces smoothing window and current backpressure state with guidance.
- Tests: unit tests for smoothing math, integration e2e that injects burst traffic and asserts reduced scale-up churn and controlled 429 behavior.

Checklist:
- [x] Design review with SRE and Runtime teams (ENG-2147)
- [x] Unit tests for smoothing and token bucket
- [x] Integration e2e simulating bursty traffic
- [x] Telemetry dashboards updated
- [x] Migration notes and rollout plan
- [x] Backwards-compatible API schema changes (defaults safe)

Implementation notes:
- EMA parameters chosen to have ~30s half-life by default; pools serving low-latency workloads can set shorter windows.
- Grace tokens default to 2 tokens per pool; a token represents ~1s worth of headroom at pool's nominal throughput.
- Backpressure is advisory: scheduler will attempt to respect backpressure_rate before rejecting; only when backpressure persists will the gateway return 429s.
- To avoid unsafe throttling loops, backpressure updates include a monotonic seq and TTL.

Breaking changes: none expected; all new fields are optional with safe defaults.

Rollout plan: staged rollout: 1) feature gated off by default; 2) enable on Canary Dedicated clusters for 48h; 3) enable for opt-in customers with console toggle; 4) enable by default after 2 weeks of stable metrics.

Migration: API customers can ignore new fields; operators upgrading on-prem should ensure controllers and gateway are updated together.

Testing:
- Unit and integration tests added under tests/e2e/dedicated_smoothing_test.go
- Load test harness reproduces 1-2 minute burst profiles and asserts scale-up events reduced by >=50% while 95th-p99 latency impact <5% in our baseline.

- 1b3d2c4: gateway: backpressure header + backchannel client

- d4f2a9f: console: show per-pool smoothing/backpressure state
- f5b6c7d: tests: unit + e2e harness for burst scenarios

- controller/autoscaler/smoothing.go: implement EMA and smoothing helpers (approx. 220 LOC)
- pkg/allocator/soft_capacity.go: grace tokens and soft capacity decisions
- gateway/backpressure/handler.go: header parsing and backchannel emitter
- api/config/v1/dedicated_pool.yaml: schema additions and docs
- telemetry/metrics_registry.go: new metric registration
- console/src/pages/pool/BackpressureCard.tsx: UI surface

- handler.go (gateway header): "if hdr := req.Header.Get(\"X-Redwood-Backpressure\"); hdr != \"\" { parseAndApply(hdr) }"

- Leah Kim (Runtime): asked for benchmark numbers for default EMA half-life; Response: added results from synthetic bursts showing 60% reduction in scale-ups with 30s half-life.
- Owen Patel (Security): requested audit logging for backpressure events; Response: added structured audit logs under telemetry/backpressure_events.
- Reviewer approvals: Marco Alvarez approved after TTL addition; Leah Kim approved after benchmark numbers; Owen Patel approved after audit logs.

Merge and post-merge actions:
- Merge method: squash and merge
- Post-merge: created a follow-up issue to add gauntlet tests against real customer workloads (ENG-2210)
- Updated canary dashboards and set alert: DedicatedPool.SmoothedUtilization > 95% for 5m -> page oncall

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: misconfigured smoothing window could delay needed scale-ups -> mitigation: default conservative half-life and admin override to disable smoothing per pool.
- Risk: backpressure loops between gateway and controller -> mitigation: monotonic seq + TTL + min-scheduling floor.

Release notes: "Improves autoscaler stability by smoothing utilization signals and introduces an advisory backpressure mechanism from gateway to controllers to reduce churn on short bursts. Operators: feature is opt-in and has safe defaults; see admin docs for tuning guidance."

title: Resumable streaming: disconnect quarantine, server timeouts, and function-call idempotency
preview:
Context and motivation: Production customers hit a class of errors where brief client disconnects (mobile network flaps, browser tab swaps) caused long-running streaming requests that invoked tools to either get duplicated or be lost after client reconnect. Existing resume tokens preserved raw streaming offsets but did not maintain function-call intent or partial outputs, making resuming tool invocations unsafe. This PR introduces a small set of changes to make streaming resumes predictable and safe for function/tool calls and structured output in the OpenAI compatibility layer.

Merge notes: squash-merged after approvals; no migration scripts required for customer data beyond session metadata backfill which is handled lazily on first resume touch.

title: Introduce Toolcall Conductor with Async Signal Latching and OpenAI-compatible Result Hydration
preview: Context: Customers increasingly run multi-tool workflows where the model issues parallel function/tool calls and expects deterministic result composition, streaming stability, and graceful retries. Motivation: previous ad-hoc orchestration led to gibberish structured outputs, duplicate side-effects when retries happened, and brittle OpenAI-compat behavior for function-calling sequences. This PR introduces a new lightweight Toolcall Conductor that centralizes execution ergonomics: async signal latching to coordinate parallel tool runs, idempotent result hydration to prevent duplicate injections, and an OpenAI-compat shim for function-calling semantics in streamed responses. Summary of changes: 1) runtime: add redwood/pkg/runtime/toolcall/conductor.go implementing a conductor that tracks call IDs, latches result signals, and emits ordered hydration events to the streaming layer. 2) api: update redwood/pkg/api/openai_compat/handlers.go to translate OpenAI-style function_call choices into conductor jobs and to consume conductor hydration events for stable streamed structured output. 3) sdk: small ergonomics helpers in redwood-sdk-python/redwood/tooling.py and redwood-sdk-typescript/src/tooling.ts to expose a non-blocking call API that wires into the conductor. 4) client compat tests and e2e flows in benchmark-lab/test/toolcall_conductor_test.go and redwood-examples/multi-tool-orchestration/ to demonstrate retry-safe replay. 5) docs: new section in redwood-docs/reference/tool-calling.md describing the conductor lifecycle and recommended retry semantics. Key behavior & guarantees: - Async Signal Latching: parallel tool executions post a transient signal with a stable ID; the conductor latches the first successful result per signal ID and deduplicates subsequent completions. - Idempotent Result Hydration: hydration objects are stamped with stable request-call identifiers and replay-safe tokens so retries or replays will not cause duplicate injections or side-effects. - Streaming Stability: the OpenAI-compat shim buffers partial tool outputs and emits structured function_call + response blocks in the same ordering across initial stream and any reconnects. - Retry policy: default hedged-retry policy with configurable window; retries for non-idempotent tools are disabled by default and require opt-in via tool metadata. Migration notes: minor SDK change for tool registration signature (opt-in param "idempotent": true/false) and server-side default policy; existing tool integrations continue to work but will not get automatic retries until annotated. Backward compatibility: OpenAI-compat endpoints remain backwards-compatible for single-tool calls; multi-tool streaming semantics improved but explicit opt-in required in some edge cases. Checklist: - [x] Implement conductor core and unit tests - [x] Integrate with OpenAI-compat handler - [x] Add SDK ergonomics helpers and examples - [x] Add regression tests for retries and replay - [x] Update docs and migration guide Testing: unit tests for latch/dedup logic, integration tests covering streaming reconnection scenarios, manual QA on Dedicated and Hosted with synthetic multi-tool workloads, load tests in perf pipeline to validate latency impact. Risk & mitigations: potential perf overhead from conductor coordination under extreme fan-out; mitigated with batching windows and fast-path bypass for single-tool calls. Non-idempotent tools present risk of duplicate side-effects; default policy disables retries and docs + SDK require explicit idempotent flag. Related work: follow-ups expected to add observability hooks in Redwood Console for per-call idempotency rates and latency heatmaps. Commits summary: Commit a1b2c3 add conductor scaffolding and interface definitions; Commit d4e5f6 implement signal latch and dedupe; Commit f7g8h9 add openai_compat translations and streaming glue; Commit i0j1k2 SDK helpers and tests; Commit l3m4n5 docs and examples. title: Policy-driven gradient rollouts and shadow/A-B traffic orchestration
preview:
Motivation: provide a single policy layer that can express canary gradients, A/B splits, shadowing, and automated rollback triggers for model variant rollouts across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. This PR implements a policy-driven rollout engine and runtime hooks to route traffic incrementally, evaluate health metrics, and trigger automated rollback or promotion when thresholds are met.

What this change contains: checklist: [x] rollout policy DSL and parser; [x] runtime router integration (routing-by-policy); [x] metrics adapter to evaluate success criteria (latency, error-rate, quality-regression hooks); [x] shadow mode implementation (non-impacting mirrored requests); [x] A/B split support with weighted sampling; [x] automated rollback circuit with hysteresis and cooldown; [x] console integration for visualizing rollout state and editing policies; [x] e2e and unit tests with synthetic traffic scenarios.

Design notes: the policy DSL is deliberately conservative: it expresses a percentage gradient (start->target), evaluation windows, primary metrics with tolerated deltas, and fallback actions. Policies are evaluated by a lightweight controller: the controller emits routing directives to the router via an atomic config push (no hot-restart required). To avoid cross-tenant leakage the controller enforces model variant isolation and integrates with existing RBAC checks.

Rollout modes supported: 1) gradient canary (0->N% over time), 2) A/B experiments with deterministic assignment keys, 3) shadow/mirror traffic where responses are recorded and compared asynchronously, 4) manual promote/abort via console.

Safety: automated rollback is conservative — thresholds must be met for three consecutive windows and the controller requires a minimum sample size before taking action. A circuit-breaker prevents rapid flapping and records reasons in audit logs. Emergency abort can be triggered via API or console and will immediately revert routing directives.

Testing and validation: included e2e scenarios that run synthetic traffic mixes (latency skew, quality regressions simulated via metric stubs). We also added perf microbenchmarks ensuring router decision path adds <0.5ms p50 on our standard config.

Migration notes: existing rollouts configured with the older mirror/canary flags are automatically translated to the new policy model on startup with a one-time migration job. Admins can opt-out via the feature flag rollout.policy_migration=false.

Operational notes: rollout controller exposes metrics: rollout_progress, rollout_failure_count, rollback_count, policy_eval_latency_ms. Alerts recommended: rollout_failure_count > 0 for 5m and policy_eval_latency_ms > 250ms.

Related: ENG-7432 implements the policy DSL and engine; ENG-7500 wires the console UI and migration job.

title: Multiplexer: parallel tool execution, idempotent retries, and ergonomic result injection
preview:
Motivation: production workloads increasingly issue multiple tool calls per response (lookups, enrichment, external actions) and clients expect predictable injection semantics, low-latency parallelism, and transparent retry behavior when external tools fail. This PR introduces a lightweight multiplexer for tool execution that: 1) executes independent tool calls in parallel when safe, 2) exposes an explicit idempotency token + retry middleware for transient tool failures, 3) provides an ergonomic result-injection API surface for handlers (deterministic injection points with ordering hints), and 4) keeps OpenAI-compatibility behaviors (function-calling fallback and structured outputs) intact.

Summary of changes: commits implement a runtime-level ToolMultiplexer that accepts a list of ToolCall requests and returns deterministic ToolResults with stable ids and insertion points. We added a RetryMiddleware that respects per-tool idempotency tokens and exponential backoff policies, and a backpressure-aware worker pool that bounds concurrency based on route config. The compatibility shim for openai->redwood function calling was updated to translate function return values into the new ToolResult shape. Documentation and SDK ergonomics were updated (Python and TS examples) and end-to-end tests were added for mixed success/failure scenarios.

title: bake signed artifact index and provenance into private-installer release manifests
preview: Motivation: private deployments (VPC/on-prem) require a reproducible, signed index that bundles release artifacts (installer tarballs, Helm charts, Terraform modules), attestations (SBOM/prov.json), and signature metadata so customers can verify what they deploy. This PR introduces a baked 'signed-artifact-index' generation step in the private-installer release pipeline, integrates Notary v2-compatible signing (via GCP KMS in CI), surfaces provenance files alongside Helm and Terraform artifacts, and wires the installer/upgrade tooling to consume the index during installs and rollbacks. Implementation summary: 1) CI: add .github/workflows/release-private-installer-index.yml that builds artifacts in a hermetic container, generates SBOMs (syft), produces provenance JSON, and invokes notary-v2 client to produce signatures and an OCI index blob that contains pointers to signed artifacts; 2) release manifests: produce release-manifests/private/<version>/signed-artifact-index.json (index is content-addressed and references OCI descriptors for artifacts plus prov and sbom); 3) installer: private-installer/installer/manifest_fetcher.go updated to fetch and verify the signed index using a configurable verification key/cert bundle; 4) helm & terraform: helm-charts/values.yaml and terraform/modules/private_release/main.tf updated to prefer signed artifacts when index is present; 5) upgrade/rollback tooling: upgrade-planer now reads index history to plan deterministic rollbacks. Checklist: - [x] CI job builds artifacts and SBOMs - [x] Notary v2 signing step implemented with KMS key reference (kept configurable per org) - [x] Signed index JSON schema and example added - [x] Installer verification path implemented and tested locally - [x] Terraform module fallback added when index absent - [x] Documentation and upgrade notes drafted (docs/private-release-index.md) Notes on security: private key material is never stored in the repo; CI uses ephemeral signing via KMS grant. For air-gapped/private builds, the index generator supports an 'offline-sign' mode where prov + sbom are exported for customer-side signing. Rollout plan: land on main behind feature flag RELEASE_SIGNED_INDEX, enable on canary private release, collect verified installs, then flip default for new releases. Related: follows up on ENG-4920 and ties into SUP-3212 customer request for verifiable artifacts. Release notes: see release_notes field.

title: v1/chat.completions: resilient stream-fragmenting + truncated-logprobs + toolcall backoff
preview:
Motivation: We've observed two classes of production failures in the OpenAI /v1/chat.completions compatibility adapter when tool/function calling and long-running model responses are involved: 1) clients receiving partial or duplicated streamed chunks after transient network blips or internal retries, and 2) malformed structured outputs (JSON) emitted incrementally by the model which break downstream tool parsing. Additionally, customers running toolcall heavy workloads saw elevated tail error rates when the function invocation subsystem experienced transient 5xxs or long latencies.

This PR implements a set of protocol and runtime changes to improve UX and parity with typical OpenAI semantics while keeping streaming stable:
- Stream fragment reconciliation: attach a "stream_fragment_id" UUID to every top-level event in the stream envelope and an optional "resume_token" when resuming. The client-side SDKs can re-assemble idempotent fragments and drop duplicates after reconnect. This reduces double-insert issues observed in clients that reconnect mid-response.
- Truncated logprobs envelope: instead of omitting logprobs for long token runs, we add a "logprobs_truncated": true flag and a "last_token_index" field so clients can reconcile partial probability data without mis-attributing tokens. This preserves parity with expected OpenAI logprobs semantics for downstream evaluators.
- Toolcall backoff and fallback policy: the toolcall manager now implements exponential backoff with jitter for transient tool failures (5xx/timeout). After 3 retries or 10s cumulative delay we emit a structured fallback event and mark the function call as "failed_retryable" in the response envelope. Admin-configurable policy allows immediate fallback to a non-tool resolution path (e.g., model-only response) for customers that prefer availability over tool fidelity.
- Structured output sanitizer: streaming JSON fragments are validated by a permissive streaming parser. We now buffer up to 4KB of partial JSON fragments per toolcall and attempt best-effort correction for common issues (trailing commas, unclosed braces) before sending final structured outputs. If sanitizer cannot repair, we emit a "structured_output_error" event with the raw fragment for downstream diagnostics.
- Client heartbeat: server emits a small "heartbeat" control event every 18s on long streams to keep HTTP/HTTP2 intermediaries alive and to surface stuck connections earlier to clients.

- API/compat: redwood/api/compat/chatv1_adapter.py -- add fragment_id/resume_token header handling, map truncated logprobs into new envelope keys, and wire toolcall fallback flags.
- Runtime: redwood/runtime/streaming/fragment_coordinator.go -- fragment generation and dedupe table + resume token handling.
- Tooling: redwood/runtime/tooling/toolcall_manager.go -- retry/backoff policy, fallback event emission, metrics counters.
- Observability: redwood/observability/metrics/streaming_metrics.promcfg -- new metrics: stream.fragment.reconciled, toolcall.retry.count, structured_output.errors.
- SDKs: redwood-sdk-python/streaming_client.py -- resume token logic + heartbeat handling; redwood-sdk-typescript/streaming.ts -- same. Tests added to both SDKs.
- Tests: unit tests for fragment coordinator, integration test that simulates mid-stream disconnect and verifies idempotent reconstruction, e2e canary with perf-canary harness.

title: dynamic per-channel requant + KV-aware jitter-resilient continuous-batching
preview: Background and motivation: Production workloads show variance in per-channel activation distributions across prompts and token positions which makes static requant scales suboptimal. We observed systematic p99 latency spikes when the runtime switched between hot and cold KV cache footprints under continuous-batching; these were exacerbated by small batching jitter when multiple streams aligned. This PR implements a dynamic per-channel requant stage combined with a KV-cache-aware scheduling hint and a jitter-resilient continuous-batching policy to smooth throughput while keeping low tail latency. Scope: 1) A lightweight per-channel scale estimator that runs on the microkernel hot path and updates requant multipliers at configurable amortization windows. 2) A cheap, lock-free KV cache sizing hint that the scheduler uses to avoid abrupt prefix evictions during batch coalescing. 3) A jitter-resilient continuous-batcher that introduces minimal, adaptive hold windows to align requests from high-variance sources without increasing median latency. 4) Microkernel changes to expose a fused transform that applies requant multipliers inline with dequant/attn input packing to eliminate an extra copy. Design highlights and tradeoffs: - Estimator runs every N tokens (configurable per model) and uses exponential moving moments to remain stable across bursts. The goal is to reduce per-token compute overhead to <0.5% while adapting scales within 5-10k tokens. - Scheduler hint is advisory: it tags requests with estimated KV working set delta. The placement layer still enforces hard SLOs; the hint avoids placing requests that would cause a working-set jump on the same GPU within a short window. - Batcher employs an adaptive jitter budget: it only delays the head of a queue by up to X ms if the expected throughput improvement outweighs the added latency; X decays if p99 latency rises. Benchmarks (representative): - OPT-13B, INT8 hybrid (smoothquant + per-channel dynamic re-quant): throughput +10.8% on steady-state mixed sized prompts, median latency +2% (due to micro-batching alignment), p99 latency -14% on tail-heavy traces. - Llama-2-7B FP8 fallback: end-to-end token cost reduced by ~7% due to fewer KV evictions and reduced memory copies. - End-to-end e2e test with 500 simulated concurrent streams: observed 8-12% lower GPU-side memcpy time and 6-9% better sustained throughput. Implementation notes: - Runtime changes: src/runtime/quant/dyn_requant.h/cpp, src/runtime/quant/microkernel_requant.s, src/runtime/scheduler/kv_hint_scheduler.cpp - Batcher: src/runtime/batching/jitter_batcher.cpp, with a new config block in configs/batching.yaml - Tests: tests/quant/test_dyn_requant.cc (unit), bench/latency/bench_kv_jitter.py (integration traces) - Docs: docs/perf/quantization.md updated with configuration knobs and suggested Linear tasks for tuning. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for estimator accuracy and stability - [x] Integration benchmark harness with trace replay - [x] Backwards-compatibility: dynamic requant disabled by default; opt-in via runtime config - [x] Release notes draft included - [x] SRE smoke test run on dedicated cluster and passed Notes on safety and compatibility: This change touches kernel hot-paths; dynamic scaling is guarded behind a runtime flag and tuned conservatively out-of-the-box to avoid regression. We included an automatic rollback condition in the scheduler when p99 exceeds baseline by 20% during an experiment. Related work: ENG-2048 (per-channel requant research spike), PR #612345 (microkernel packing refactor) for reference. Next steps: 1) A follow-up PR to expose an admin dashboard for per-model scale drift metrics and recommended amortization windows. 2) Experiment with a learned estimator offline and compare to the EMA-based estimator used here.

title: hybrid flashattn + lazy-kv-eviction + operator-fusion for long-seq workloads
preview:
Motivation: Production workloads with mixed short- and long-context requests show uneven tail latency and high aggregate token cost due to conservative KV cache retention and generic attention kernel selection. This PR introduces a hybrid attention path that (1) dispatches FlashAttention when safe, (2) uses a lightweight fused matmul-add-dropout kernel for mid-sized heads, and (3) falls back to the existing fallback kernel for pathological shapes. In parallel, we implement a lazy KV eviction policy that decouples KV entry lifetime from request completion and enables opportunistic eviction under memory pressure. Together these changes reduce long-sequence p99 latency and improve throughput for streaming workloads while keeping memory bounded.

What changed (high level): implemented a Flash-aware scheduler that picks kernel variant at invocation time based on sequence length, head size, and kv-cache residency; added a fused QKV->matmul+add kernel for medium shapes (mp size 16/32) to reduce kernel launch overhead; integrated a lazy-kv-eviction manager that uses LRU+age heuristics and signals the scheduler to prefer compact kernels when eviction is active; CI/build: updated CUDA kernels, operator registration, and added unit/integration tests; docs: runtime tuning doc and rollout checklist.

Benchmarks (representative): evaluated on 8xA100-80GB and local dev GPU (A5000). Long-seq streaming (seq=16384, batch=1) shows p50 latency -12%, p95 -23%, p99 -31%, tokens/sec +18% vs main with previous FlashAttention dispatch. Medium mixed workload (seq distribution 128-2048) throughput improved +12% and reduced kernel launch counts by ~28%. Memory: average KV memory footprint reduced ~9% due to lazy eviction behavior under synthetic tail traffic.

Testing & CI: unit tests for kernel selection logic, KV eviction invariants, and end-to-end streaming harness. Added synthetic replay tests for real production traces. CI initially failed on quantization edge-case in fused kernel (nvcc error for sm_80 path), fixed by tightening alignments and adding memcpy fallback for odd strides. Full CI now green.

Rollout plan: feature-gated behind runtime.experimental.hybrid_attention. Staged rollout: Canary on Dedicated pool -> 10% Hosted -> 50% -> full. Canary will enable additional tracing hooks for per-request kernel decisions and kv-eviction events. Rollback path: flip feature flag or restart runtime to main kernel selection. The PR does not change public API, no breaking change.

Checklist: - [x] Kernel selection heuristics implemented and unit-tested - [x] Fused kernel validated on sm_70/75/80 - [x] Lazy KV eviction has safety invariants and metrics - [x] End-to-end perf harness and regression alerts - [x] Rollout feature flag and tracing - [x] Docs & tuning guide updated

title: Introduce token-lifecycle burnup histogram, cross-service latency fingerprint, and alert coalescer
preview:
Context: token-level SLO noise and long-tail cost/latency alerts are creating alert fatigue. Existing token-cost rollups capture per-token aggregates but struggle to: (1) show how cost accumulates across pipeline stages (ingest, model, postprocess), (2) identify cross-service latency shape changes (same total latency but different phase breakdown), and (3) suppress repeated similar alerts that indicate the same incident.

What this PR does: introduces three coordinated pieces:
1) token_lifecycle_burnup histogram metric (metric/token_lifecycle_burnup): records cumulative token cost/latency at stage boundaries per invocation (labels: tenant_id, model, route, stage). Supports configurable buckets and exported rollups.
2) latency_fingerprint: a small vectorized signature (string) derived from percentile deltas across stages for an invocation; stored as a tag on traces and emitted as a low-cardinality rollup for fast grouping.
3) alert_coalescer: a server-side component in observability-alerts that groups token-level alerts using latency_fingerprint similarity and recent-window dedupe rules (window default 5m, jittered). Coalescer emits a single incident with aggregated counts and exemplar trace links.

Design notes:
- token_lifecycle_burnup is implemented as a histogram with predefined exponential buckets plus explicit tail buckets to capture heavy-tailed token cost. The SDK helper is backwards-compatible (adds a single call at stage boundaries).
- latency_fingerprint is intentionally low-cardinality (bucketized percentiles -> base64-encoded 64-bit signature) to avoid index/explosion. We ensure privacy by hashing tenant_id before attaching higher-cardinality metadata.
- Alert coalescer runs as an idempotent stateless podset backed by a short-lived Redis lease for leader selection; it consumes alert events, computes fingerprint similarity via Hamming distance on the signature and groups alerts meeting threshold (0.8 similarity).

Rollout plan:
- Feature flag: observability.token_burnup.enabled=false by default.
- Canary: enable for 2 enterprise tenants for 48 hours, sampling rate 5% invocations.
- Ramp: 5% -> 25% -> 100% over 2 weeks with monitoring on incident changes and index size.

Checklist:
- [x] Metric schema and SDK helper
- [x] Tracing enrichment for fingerprint
- [x] Alert coalescer service + leader election
- [x] Dashboards: token-burnup panel, fingerprint cohort explorer
- [x] Runbook: how to un-coalesce and escalate
- [x] Unit + integration tests

Why this is safe: no breaking API changes. All new signals are behind a feature flag and low-sample default. The coalescer is additive — it suppresses alert noise but can be disabled quickly.

Related: ENG-4821 (alert noise reduction), ENG-4899 (token cost attribution).
