title: Improve chat stream framing, function-call handshake, and logprob envelope
preview:
Motivation: We observed intermittent corruption of streamed messages when function calls were emitted mid-chunk and when stop sequences split across buffer boundaries. Consumers (third-party SDKs and internal examples) reported mis-attributed logprobs and truncated function-call payloads. This causes downstream tooling that relies on deterministic final outputs and token-level diagnostics to misbehave.

Changes in this PR: added a lightweight framing state machine to the streaming runtime so every SSE/data chunk is annotated with a stable boundary token and an explicit event for function-call lifecycle: 'fn_call.start' and 'fn_call.end'. This removes ambiguity about when a tool invocation begins and ends across stream re-fragments. To surface token-level diagnostics without polluting the incremental stream we emit a final 'logprob_envelope' JSON object as the last event for both streaming and non-streaming requests; it maps token offsets to per-role logprobs and is documented as a separate (optional) payload consumers can request.

Backward compatibility: The new framing is opt-out for existing clients by default. We add a query parameter include_logprob_envelope (true|false) and a legacy alias logprobs that maps to the same behavior for OpenAI-style callers. Existing simple callers that don't parse the terminal envelope see no change. The handshake events are idempotent and can be ignored by clients that only read incremental content.

Behavioral guarantees: stop sequence handling has been unified: when a stop token spans a chunk boundary the runtime defers finalization until the full stop is observed and then emits the terminal event in the same atomic sequence across streaming and non-streaming modes. Function-call arguments are emitted as a single atomic object once the 'fn_call.end' event occurs. The logprob envelope is computed using the final KV-cache state to ensure token logprobs are consistent with the produced text.

Performance and safety: microbenchmarks show a small median latency regression (~2%) for small requests due to additional framing metadata, but a ~12% reduction in upstream token compute in workloads that reuse prefixes because of improved KV-cache reuse in the stream path. All code paths guard against exposing private keys or internal tracing identifiers in the emitted envelope.

Tests and verification: added unit tests for framing transitions, integration tests that simulate chunked TCP delivery and function-call mid-stream, and e2e tests verifying parity between streaming and blocking endpoints on stop/logprob outputs. CI initially failed the integration matrix due to a flaky test that assumed synchronous framing; that test was updated and the CI rerun passed.

Docs and examples: added a short migration note and an examples recipe showing how SDKs should collect 'fn_call.start'/'fn_call.end' events and parse the final 'logprob_envelope' for diagnostics.

Notes for reviewers: major areas to focus on are the correctness of framing transitions under network re-fragmentation, the atomicity of function-call object emission, and the memory footprint of the logprob envelope on very long responses. The PR is intentionally conservative in toggles to allow gradual rollout and feature-flagged experiments.

title: typed errors + ergonomic iterable reconnect API across SDKs
preview:
Motivation: Developers repeatedly hit vague runtime errors when streams failed (auth handshake, transient network) and had to implement ad-hoc parsing logic to decide whether to retry, resume, or surface an error. This PR introduces language-native typed error classes across the three SDKs (Python/TypeScript/Go) and a unified ergonomic iterable reconnect API for stream consumers.

What this change does: 
- Introduces TypedError/StreamError subclasses with machine-friendly error_code, retriable, and suggestion fields across SDKs.
- Adds a new StreamIterable API (python: StreamIterable, ts: createStreamIterable, go: StreamIterator) that yields typed result objects and automatically attempts a single reconnect on retriable failures by default with backoff tuning. Reconnect behavior is configurable by the caller.
- Deprecates low-level sync callback hooks (on_chunk/on_error) in favor of async iterables/promises/iterators to improve ergonomics and parity.
- Adds structured telemetry hooks (opt-in) so apps can key metrics by error_code without brittle string matching.
- Small refactors: move common auth-retry utility into internal/sdk-common, update changelogs and bump SDK package versions.

Backward compatibility and migration: 
- Old callback APIs are still present but marked deprecated; they will emit one deprecation log per process and are scheduled for removal in the next major. Migration notes are added to each SDK CHANGELOG.
- Typed error shapes are additive; existing code catching broad exceptions will continue to work but can opt-in to typed handling for clearer logic.

Testing and rollout: 
- Unit tests added for typed error construction, serialization, and iterable reconnect edge cases. Integration tests simulate transient auth failures with a test double of the runtime.
- Canary rollout to Dedicated region us-west-2 for 72 hours before full merge.

Checklist: 
- [x] Python implementation and tests
- [x] TypeScript implementation and tests
- [x] Go implementation and tests
- [x] Changelogs and version bumps
- [x] Docs snippet in quickstarts repo pointing to new API example

References: Linear ENG-1987, SUP-402 (customer-facing incident), internal design doc: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-1987/stream-reconnect-typed-errors (internal link).

title: concurrent-tool-runner: parallel tool invocation + OpenAI function_call compatibility updates
preview: Motivation: customers increasingly chain multiple tool calls per turn and want lower tail latency by running compatible tools in parallel, plus predictable result injection and idempotent retries. This PR introduces a server-side concurrent tool runner, extends the OpenAI-compatible function_call surface to allow explicit parallel calls and tool-result references, and updates SDK helpers and streaming merge semantics to keep the API stable for existing clients. Summary of changes: 1) runtime: add services/inference/tool_runner/concurrent_runner with a pluggable execution strategy (sequential | parallel | hybrid), a run_id-based idempotency layer for retries, and deterministic merge ordering for structured output injection. 2) api/openai-compat: extend function_call payload with optional keys: "parallel_calls" (array of tool names), "tool_result_refs" (map-like list of result identifiers), and "injection_policy" (deterministic | merge_stream | attach_raw). The compat layer translates these into internal execution plans and exposes merged streaming boundaries to callers. 3) streaming: improve chunk merging to reduce interleaving artifacts when multiple tool streams are active; add a small backpressure window to avoid head-of-line blocking in multi-tool flows. 4) SDKs: python & typescript helpers to create and await parallel tool futures, helpers to submit a single composite call that the server will execute in parallel when possible, and a deterministic merge utility for client-side local execution. 5) docs & tests: update openapi spec, add e2e test harness that exercises parallel function calls, and add unit tests covering retry idempotency and injection ordering. Backwards compatibility: default behavior remains sequential tool execution; the new keys are opt-in. Also preserved the original function_call semantics for single-tool usage to avoid breaking existing OpenAI-compatible clients. Rationale: moving parallelism into the platform lets us optimize batching and GPU utilization while keeping SDK ergonomics simple; adding run_id/idempotency reduces duplicate side-effects for retried calls. Rollout plan: feature gated behind runtime flag concurrent_tool_runner.enabled. Initially enabled in staging and can be opt-in per-tenant via config; will be enabled by default after 2 weeks of telemetry. Related: linked_linear ENG-4123 ENG-4181. Notes: this PR intentionally avoids introducing a new RPC surface; changes are translations within the compat layer and runtime to minimize client churn.

title: Add DMA stall & kernel-latency counters with sigprof-compatible timeline exporter
preview:
Context: We need finer-grained, low-overhead indicators of where inference stalls occur inside the serving runtime so that model routing, batching and kernel-selection heuristics can be tuned without attaching heavy profilers. This PR adds DMA-stall counters collected from the GPU driver telemetry shim, per-kernel latency histograms sampled into buckets, and a compact timeline exporter that emits SigProf-compatible records that downstream dashboards (and our internal perfboard) can ingest. Motivation: several customer incidents pointed to intermittent pauses that didn't show up in token-level latency traces (ENG-1223). Existing CPU-side sampling catches high-level stalls but misses kernel queuing and DMA scheduling hotspots.

Summary of changes (high level):
- runtime/telemetry: new dma_counters.{h,cc} implementing low-overhead atomic counters for DMA queue stalls and preemption events.
- runtime/profiler: kernel_latency_histogram, continuous bucketing with sliding-window reset and exponential decay.
- tools/timeline_exporter: sigprof_exporter that converts our internal samples to a compact record stream (.sprec) compatible with SigProf importers.
- benchmarks/microbench: added microbench harness that simulates multi-model mixed workloads and emits representative traces for dashboard ingestion.
- docs/observability: brief guidance and sample Grafana dashboard JSON for the latency buckets and DMA-stall heatmap.

Commits (summary):
1) Add dma counter primitives and basic increment paths (mchen)
2) Wire counter increments in queueing/scheduler path and kernel dispatch (mchen)
3) Implement kernel_latency_histogram with lockless circular buffers (mchen)
4) Add sigprof-compatible exporter and small CLI tool to emit .sprec from live socket (mchen)
5) Microbench harness + example dashboard JSON (mchen)
6) Tests, comments, CI adjustments and small flake fixes (mchen)

Files changed (representative):
- runtime/telemetry/dma_counters.h
- runtime/telemetry/dma_counters.cc
- runtime/profiler/kernel_latency_histogram.h
- runtime/profiler/kernel_latency_histogram.cc
- runtime/scheduler/scheduler_dispatch.cc (insert increments at dispatch path, ~12 LOC)
- tools/timeline_exporter/sigprof_exporter.cc
- benchmarks/microbench/sigprof_dmamicro.cxx
- docs/observability/sigprof-dma-dashboard.json
- tests/telemetry/test_dma_counters.cc

Small illustrative diff (scheduler hook):
- // before: dispatch kernel
+ // after: increment DMA stall counter if queue detected busy
+ dma_counters::global()->maybe_increment_dma_stall(queue_id);

Design notes and tradeoffs: we intentionally use a very small number of atomic counters and compact histogram buckets to keep per-request overhead sub-200ns on hot paths. The histogram is sampled on kernel completion (not per token) to avoid touching hot token paths. We opted for SigProf record compatibility instead of perfetto to broaden downstream tool options and because SigProf's compact record model reduces exporter overhead.

Bench results (CI microbench + local runs):
- Baseline (no counters): median dispatch latency 74us, p99 1.9ms
- With counters + exporter enabled (sampling only): median dispatch latency 77us (+4%), p99 2.0ms (+5%)
- Continuous microbench (mixed sequence lengths) shows DMA-stall counter correlation with p99 spikes in the kernel queue, enabling reliable detection in dashboards.

Test coverage: unit tests for counter increment/decrement and histogram bucketing. Integration test that runs microbench and verifies exporter produces valid .sprec frames and dashboard import script accepts them.

Review thread (high level):
- Liam: requested stronger guarantees about lockless buffer memory reclamation. Author replied with a short RFC link and added a single-writer circular buffer design and explicit hazard pointer comment.
- Priya: asked for clearer docs on how to enable exporter in Dedicated vs Private deployments. Author added docs/observability section and a note about disabling exporter by default (env var REDWOOD_SIGPROF_EXPORT=0).
- Ethan: ran local scale test and flagged a spurious flake in the microbench; author fixed timing window and re-ran CI.

CI and flakiness: initial CI run showed a flaky test in benchmarks/microbench that failed under the faster runner. I added a 2x timeout margin and retry in CI. Final CI: all checks passed (unit, integration, lint, clang-tidy). Exporter binary also passes a lightweight fuzzer for input validation.

Operational notes for SRE/Platform:
- Exporter is disabled by default in production clusters. To enable for debugging, set REDWOOD_SIGPROF_EXPORT=1 and point REDWOOD_SIGPROF_SOCKET to the collector. Exporter uses a single UDP socket and rotates files every 10s by default.
- Counters are exposed at /metrics under the telemetry namespace with the following names: redwood_dma_stall_total, redwood_kernel_latency_bucket{le=\"...\"}.
- Retention: internal decay policy keeps histograms for 10 minutes by default to avoid unbounded memory growth.

Rollout plan: canary on a single Dedicated fleet for 24h, monitor increased p95/p99 and any errors from exporter. If p99 increases by >10% revert feature flag. The PR includes a rollout script in scripts/telemetry_canary.sh.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation
- [x] Unit tests
- [x] Integration tests
- [x] Documentation added
- [x] Benchmarks + dashboards
- [x] Rollout plan

Related work: ENG-1309 (continuous batching improvements), ENG-1223 (incident follow-up), internal perfboard importers. This complements the existing tracing probes by providing compact, event-focused counters that make causal analysis easier in dashboards.

Merge details: squash-and-merge after final approvals. Merge commit message includes a short summary and links to dashboards and the microbench harness. Merged by Priya on 2026-02-26.

If you want to reproduce locally: see benchmarks/microbench/README.md — instructions to build the microbench, spin a local collector (scripts/local_sigprof_collector.py) and import the resulting .sprec into the perfboard test dashboard.

title: Capacity-aware SLO relaxation with regional soft-eviction safeguard
preview: Context: We saw repeated cascade evictions during multi-region capacity spikes where strict SLO enforcement triggered aggressive circuit breakers, causing higher tail latency and customer throttles (SUP-9812). This PR introduces a capacity-aware, timebound SLO relaxation layer plus a regional soft-eviction safeguard that stages evictions to avoid synchronized mass-drains. What changed: 1) control/allocator: add 'capacity-aware slo_relaxation' policy that dynamically relaxes non-critical SLOs by a configurable percentage when sustained node-utilization exceeds a threshold for a hold window; 2) routing/eviction: introduce 'soft-eviction' state where instances are marked for probabilistic eviction over a decay window instead of immediate hard drain, enabling load to bleed off while respecting per-tenant priority buckets; 3) telemetry: new metrics (slo_relaxation.active, soft_eviction.pending, soft_eviction.success_rate) and traces for decision points; 4) ops: feature flag rts.slo_relaxation_v1 and runtime knobs for threshold/decay; 5) tests: end-to-end canary harness and unit coverage for decision logic. Motivation: allow graceful degradation that is both capacity- and cost-aware: we prefer slight SLO slack for low-priority requests over wholesale region failovers or bulk evictions which amplify disruption. Implementation notes: the relaxation algorithm is conservative by default (5% token latency budget increase, 2-minute hold window) and respects tenant-priority masks; the soft-eviction uses jittered timers and a token-bucket backoff to limit eviction rate per-region. Operation guidance: defaults are safe for most clusters; ops can tune threshold and relaxation_pct per-capacity class. Checklist: - [x] design doc linked (internal design doc: https://linear.app/ENG/1428/capacity-aware-slo-relaxation) - [x] unit tests - [x] integration canary in perf-lab - [x] metrics + dashboard patches - [x] feature-flag gating - [x] rollout playbook attached (see PR artifacts) Commits: 3 commits (initial implementation, metrics + tests, docs + rollback hooks). High-level changed files: control/allocator/policy_manager.go, control/allocator/slo_relaxation.go, routing/eviction/soft_eviction.go, routing/eviction/soft_eviction_test.go, telemetry/metrics_definitions.yaml, ops/feature_flags.yaml, docs/ops/slo-relaxation.md, examples/perf-canary/config.yml. Review conversation summary: Marcus Lin: "Can we make tenant-priority mask evaluation O(1) to avoid runtime cost?" — Author: "Updated mask cache to constant-time lookup and added microbenchmark (see commit 2)." Aisha Patel: "How do we avoid oscillation between relaxed and strict states under flapping load?" — Author: "Added hold window and exponential backoff on relaxation decay; documented rationale and added unit tests for flapping scenario." Ethan Zhao: "Suggest lowering default relaxation_pct to 3% for small regions." — Author: "Left default at 5% but exposed knob and wrote guidance; lowered default for regions with <8 GPUs in ops playbook." CI history: Initial run failed lint due to unused import in soft_eviction_test.go; fixed in commit 3. Integration perf canary showed reduced peak eviction rate in staging (40% -> 7%) with 1.8% median latency increase on low-priority traffic. Rollout plan: enable feature flag in staging for 24h, enable in one EU region with canary traffic split 5%, monitor slo_relaxation.active, soft_eviction.success_rate, region_eviction_rate and rollback in 30m if region_eviction_rate increases. Merge: squashed and merged after approvals from all reviewers and green CI. Post-merge actions: deploy to dedicated canary clusters, coordinate on-call rotation for first 48h, add alert thresholds to SLO dashboard. Notes: This is an operationally-sensitive change; although not a breaking API change, treat rollout as a minor incident risk and follow ops playbook.

title: introduce-server-timebox-and-idempotent-cancel-fallbacks-for-streaming-compat
preview: Context: production customers observed cases where long lived streaming sessions (tool calls + structured output) were left in indeterminate states after a client network disconnect or a client-initiated Cancel. Motivation: we need a predictable server-side safety net that bounds resource residency time, ensures tool-call idempotency when retries/resume occur, and provides a clear fallback path (trimmed response + final event) that OpenAI-compatible clients can rely on. This PR implements a server-side streaming timebox (configurable per-route) that gracefully converts oversized/inflight streams into a final 'timeboxed' terminal event, augments client-cancel handling to be idempotent across reconnect/resume windows, and plumbs a compact resume token that includes tool-call idempotency metadata. Summary of changes: 1) stream manager: add configurable timebox_ms and a deterministic finalization path that emits a structured terminal event ("__redwood_timeboxed__") with a compact summary; 2) openai adapter: interpret Cancel events as idempotent markers tied to resume tokens; retries within the resume window will deduplicate tool calls using a request-level id; 3) tooling: map function-call invocations to idempotency keys and persist minimal call metadata to the stream checkpoint to support safe resume; 4) API: extend the OpenAI-compat streaming spec with two optional fields in the final event (timeboxed=true, resume_hint) without breaking existing clients; 5) tests & docs: integration tests for flaky network simulated disconnects and release notes for the behavior and recommended operational knobs. Rollout plan: feature guarded behind a runtime flag and route-level config. Backwards compatibility: default behavior is unchanged unless a route opts-in. Checklist: [x] unit tests for stream finalizer [x] integration test that simulates disconnect + resume [x] docs page and compatibility notes [x] change log entry. Related: ENG-1023 (stream lifecycle SLO), SUP-4572 (customer incident replicant).

Summary of changes: 1) stream manager: add configurable timebox_ms and a deterministic finalization path that emits a structured terminal event ("__redwood_timeboxed__") with a compact summary; 2) openai adapter: interpret Cancel events as idempotent markers tied to resume tokens; retries within the resume window will deduplicate tool calls using a request-level id; 3) tooling: map function-call invocations to idempotency keys and persist minimal call metadata to the stream checkpoint to support safe resume; 4) API: extend the OpenAI-compat streaming spec with two optional fields in the final event (timeboxed=true, resume_hint) without breaking existing clients; 5) tests & docs: integration tests for flaky network simulated disconnects and release notes for the behavior and recommended operational knobs. Rollout plan: feature guarded behind a runtime flag and route-level config. Backwards compatibility: default behavior is unchanged unless a route opts-in. Checklist: [x] unit tests for stream finalizer [x] integration test that simulates disconnect + resume [x] docs page and compatibility notes [x] change log entry. Related: ENG-1023 (stream lifecycle SLO), SUP-4572 (customer incident replicant).

title: multi-tool-execution: ergonomic API + parallel tool runner + result injection + retry policies
preview: Motivation: Improve ergonomics and reliability for tool/function-calling in the OpenAI-compat layer and native Redwood API. Customers asked for easy multi-tool invocations, deterministic injection of tool results into continuations, safe parallel tool execution, and more robust retry/backoff for transient tool failures. This PR introduces a small, backwards-compatible API extension plus a new parallel tool-runner implementation and runtime wiring for result injection and structured output hints. Summary of changes: 1) API: add `tools` invocation signature that supports multiple tool calls in a single step and an optional `parallel` flag. 2) Runtime: implement ParallelToolRunner with optimistic concurrency, coordinated token streaming, and ordered result injection points. 3) Retry semantics: add configurable retry/backoff policies per-tool with circuit-breaking thresholds and idempotency markers. 4) Structured output: expand function-calling schema to include `injection_point` and `merge_strategy` hints for deterministic consolidation of parallel results. 5) OpenAI compatibility: surface the new multi-tool signature through the OpenAI-compat endpoint with feature gating and compatibility warning headers. 6) SDK: Python and TS client updates for ergonomics and typed helpers. 7) Tests & docs: unit + integration tests for parallel tool execution, backpressure scenarios, and streaming stability; docs and migration guide. Implementation notes and tradeoffs: - Parallel execution is best-effort: by default the runner executes tools concurrently and injects results in the order of `injection_point` when present. If tools have cross-dependencies, callers should use explicit sequential mode or `depends_on` fields. - Streaming: we added a coordinator that merges streaming outputs from active tools into a single response stream while preserving token interleaving semantics. This required a small amount of additional buffering (configurable via runtime flags) to avoid head-of-line blocking. - Retries: only idempotent-marked tools will be automatically retried; non-idempotent tools can opt into safe-retry wrappers. Retries use exponential backoff with jitter and a per-tool max attempt limit. - Backwards compatibility: existing function-calling calls remain unchanged. The new fields are optional and gated behind an SDK flag and an OpenAI-compat header for the next release window. Checklist: - [x] Design doc reviewed by runtime and API teams (ENG-8341) - [x] Prototype validated in perf-canary with small synthetic workload - [x] Unit tests for ParallelToolRunner and retry policies - [x] Integration tests for streaming merge and structured injection - [x] Python + TS SDK helpers + docs snippets - [x] Compatibility guard and migration notes in docs - [x] Load test showing <5% median latency regression on typical chat workloads Known limitations: - Result consolidation for very large tool outputs can increase memory usage; we recommend streaming-friendly tool outputs or chunking. - Deterministic ordering across parallel calls is only guaranteed when callers provide explicit injection points. Related: ENG-8341 (tool ergonomics), PM-412 (product requirements), SUP-2023 (support case that drove retries). See inline changed files for details.

title: dynamic per-route payload limits + streaming proxy buffer tuning to stabilize function-calls and OpenAI-compatible chunking
preview:
Motivation: Several production routes (chat-stream, tools/function_call, and OpenAI-compat proxying) have experienced intermittent stalls and truncated function_call payloads when large intermediate tool outputs are streamed in small chunks. Root cause summary: a) statically-sized streaming proxy flush thresholds and buffer headroom do not match per-route payload patterns; b) global payload size limit was too coarse (caused safe-failures for tool outputs but allowed oversized non-streamed requests); c) OpenAI compatibility shim assumed final-message framing which breaks when intermediate buffer flushes split function_call JSON tokens across frames.

What this PR does (summary): Adds a dynamic per-route payload limiter and adaptive streaming-proxy buffer tuning. The gateway now derives two parameters per route: effective_payload_limit (bytes) and buffer_flush_threshold (bytes). Derivation uses route metadata (route type: chat|tooling|embedding), model family hints, and historical size percentiles (configurable window). To preserve OpenAI compatibility we introduce a small "reassemble-on-boundary" shim that guarantees function_call JSON objects are not emitted mid-object across chunk boundaries when possible (best-effort with a soft timeout).

Key changes (high level): - gateway/routes/policy: new PolicyWeights to compute effective_payload_limit per-route and expose policy endpoint for manual overrides. - gateway/proxy/stream_buffer: adaptive buffer with three modes: passthrough (low-latency), accumulate (when API route signals structured-output), and reassemble (OpenAI function_call safety window). - gateway/openai/compat: added streaming-function-call-shim that watches for "function_call" object starts and attempts to flush complete tokens when buffer thresholds are hit. - metrics and observability: new metrics gateway.route.payload_limit_bytes and gateway.proxy.reassemble_invocations with tagging by route/model. - docs: gateway/operational.md updated with rollout and a knob to pin per-route limits. - tests: new integration streaming test covering fragmented function_call frames and a perf benchmark for latency/cost tradeoffs.

Behavioral notes / compatibility: - Default behavior is conservative: effective_payload_limit defaults to previous global limit, only routes with historical large-percentile traffic will increase limits. This avoids surprising regressions for customers who rely on strict per-request quotas. - OpenAI compatibility: shim is best-effort to avoid emitting function_call JSON mid-object. In extremely high-throughput scenarios, the shim will fallback to emitting partial frames with a diagnostic trace header; clients relying on precise framing should be advised to rehydrate frames. - Tooling: tool outputs larger than effective_payload_limit will be rejected with a 413-like structured error that includes guidance and a route-specific suggested limit. - Operators can set route annotations to force bypassing reassembly for ultra-low-latency workloads.

Testing and verification: Unit tests for buffer edge cases and policy computation. Integration tests simulate chunked streaming where function_call JSON is split across many small token frames. Canary rollout: feature gates enabled for 10% of traffic for 24 hours before ramping to 100% with metrics checks for increased tail latency and reassemble errors.

Benchmarks: Measured on staging with a mix of 50% chat-stream, 30% function_call, 20% tooling: - 95th percentile latency for chat-stream increased by +6ms on average when reassembly mode is active; - Tail error rate for truncated function_call decreased from 0.9% -> 0.02%; - Cost per token impact negligible (<0.5%) due to opportunistic passthrough mode.

Migration / rollout: - Default policies are backwards-compatible. - Operators with custom quotas should review new route-level settings; a migration script is provided to copy global quota -> per-route default. - Release notes include instructions for emergency rollback by setting gateway.feature.dynamic_payload_limits=false.

Notes for reviewers: Pay attention to race conditions in stream_buffer reassemble logic (I added a test that reproduces a previously observed race using channel-backed flushing). The reassembly shim has a configurable timeout (default 150ms) to avoid unbounded buffering under slow producers.

title: Introduce 'Thrifty' inference profile generator for conservative batching/caching/quant presets
preview: Adds a new service+CLI to synthesize conservative, production-safe default inference profiles from short trace samples. Motivation: many customers hesitate to enable aggressive batching/quantization/caching because they lack templates that balance unit-economics wins with latency/regression risk. This change introduces 'thrifty' profiles that err on the side of lower cost while preserving tail-latency SLOs. Key pieces: 1) trace-sampler: lightweight offline analyzer that ingests representative request traces and outputs distribution summaries (seq len, token mix, prefix reuse). 2) profile-generator: rules engine that maps trace summaries to a prioritized preset (batch size, prefetch window, prefix cache policy, int8/4 quantization candidate with safety fences). 3) rw-profile-gen CLI and Docker image for customers to run locally or in infra pipelines. 4) integration with recommendations API so Console can surface the generated profile and a one-click apply to dedicated/private deployments. The generator targets conservative defaults (microbatching on high-latency tails, small prefix caches for low-reuse workloads, disabled quant for short sequences) and emits an explainable rationale for each knob. Includes unit tests, e2e smoketest in the perf-canary harness, and docs for operators. This PR intentionally avoids aggressive heuristics (no automatic live rollouts) — it emits profiles as suggestions and supports a manual canary apply flow. Related: ENG-10233 (profile UX), SUP-3281 (customer support case triggered by runaway token costs).

title: Fold per-layer scale into inline requantizer; attention KV-cache compatibility
preview: Motivation: INT8 and FP8 paths pay a per-token cost for explicit scale/dequant round-trips in the attention kernel. This PR folds stable per-layer/chunk scaling factors into an inline requantizer inside the GEMM/attention kernels and adds compatibility glue for our attention KV-cache (so cached keys/values don't trigger extra conversions on read). Goals: reduce per-token arithmetic, improve single-stream latency, and keep cached tensors bit-compatible across quant/float fallbacks. Implementation summary: 1) introduce a lightweight FoldedScale descriptor that is emitted at model-load time and stored alongside quant metadata; 2) implement inline_requantizer.{cc,hh} which applies scale folding + rounding once on kernel entry and keeps activations in the requantized layout for the kernel duration; 3) modify attention KV load/store paths to check for FoldedScale and avoid redundant dequant->requant conversions when a cached shard already carries the folded scale tag; 4) add a guarded FP8 stochastic-round option (config flag) to preserve edge-case numerics for very small-scale layers; 5) update the continuous batcher to prefer grouping requests by compatible folded-scale signatures to maximize kernel reuse. Testing & benchmarks: unit tests added (tests/quant/scale_fold_test.cc) to validate numerical equivalence within target tolerances; end-to-end perf bench (bench/quant/bench_int8_fold.py) shows 8-14% single-stream latency reduction on A100-80GB with INT8 kernels and 6-9% throughput improvement in mixed-batch workloads when continuous batching groups by folded-scale. Backwards compatibility: when FoldedScale metadata is absent, kernels fall back to the previous explicit dequant/requant code path. Migration: model loader emits folded-scale for models that pass a short calibration pass; opt-out via REDWOOD_QUANT_NO_FOLD env var. Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked in Linear ENG-1289 - [x] Unit tests - [x] Perf benchmarks attached - [x] KV-cache compatibility glue - [x] Feature gated behind config flags and env opt-out - [x] Docs: docs/perf/quant-ops.md updated Notes: this is intentionally conservative — we avoid changing on-disk weight formats and only tag cache entries with a small folded-scale id. The approach aims to reduce runtime arithmetic without altering model fidelity. Related: ENG-1344 (continuous-batching grouping by quant-signature).

title: Instrument kernel-level latency fingerprints and introduce adaptive kernel-fingerprint alerting
preview:
Motivation: We see occasional long-tail invocation latency where root cause is GPU kernel scheduling/serialization rather than model token path. Existing observability surfaces capture per-invocation token timing but lack a compact way to correlate GPU kernel-level skew across tenants and hardware. This PR instruments low-level kernel timing as compact 'fingerprints', surfaces a kernel heatmap dashboard, and introduces an adaptive alert that suppresses noise during autoscale events.

What this PR does (high-level):
- Adds runtime span attributes: kernel_name, kernel_ms, gpu_sm_id, kernel_queue_depth. Spans are emitted by the serving runtime when kernels are dispatched.
- Emits a new metric series: telemetry.kernel_fingerprint with labels {model_family, hw_class, kernel_name, tenant_id? (opt)}, using reservoir sampling to limit cardinality.
- Adds a Kernel Heatmap dashboard (dashboards/kernel-heatmap.json) that shows per-kernel p50/p95/p99 across HW classes and time-of-day.
- Introduces an alert rule (alerts/kernel-fingerprint-drift.yaml) that computes drift against a rolling baseline and only fires when drift correlates with stable capacity (i.e., not during autoscale events) to reduce false positives.
- Adds a short-lived debug mode (env var REDWOOD_KERNEL_FINGERPRINT_DEBUG) that collects per-chunk latency samples into a ring buffer for on-demand upload during incidents.
- Adds tests for metric tagging and an integration test that mocks kernel span emission and confirms the heatmap ingestion pipeline.

Checklist:
- [x] Instrument runtime spans
- [x] Metric export + reservoir sampling
- [x] Dashboard JSON
- [x] Alert rule and suppression logic
- [x] Unit + integration tests
- [x] Docs: added a short guide in docs/observability/kernel-fingerprints.md

Commits (summary):
- Add kernel span attrs and exporter hooks (sof-2026/kernel-span-export)
- Implement reservoir sampler for kernel_fingerprint series (telemetry/core)
- Add dashboard JSON and panels for kernel heatmap and per-hw kernel table
- Add alert rule with autoscale-aware suppression and test harness
- Add docs and e2e test to simulate skew and verify suppression

Files changed (high-level):
- runtime/dispatch/kernel_instrumentation.go (new)
- telemetry/metrics/kernel_sampler.go (new)
- telemetry/pipelines/kernel_ingest_pipeline.go (modified)
- observability/dashboards/kernel-heatmap.json (new)
- observability/alerts/kernel-fingerprint-drift.yaml (new)

title: v1/chat: output coordinator + role-pruner for compact structured tool responses
preview:
Motivation: customers relying on OpenAI /v1/chat.completions compatibility have increasingly complex tool/function outputs and varied role labels from legacy clients. The streaming path was producing verbose, repeated JSON fragments for tool responses and leaking low-confidence token-level logprobs during long streams. This PR introduces an 'output coordinator' layer in the v1 chat adapter and a conservative 'role-pruner' to normalize incoming roles and emit a compact structured mode for tool/function outputs.

Summary of changes: added an adapter-level coordinator that: 1) recognizes and collapses repeated tool JSON fragments into a single compact structure when clients request compact output (new query param compact_output=true), 2) supplies a configurable stop_hint_priority so that explicit stop sequences sent by the API call are respected ahead of model-internal heuristics when desired, and 3) filters streaming logprob deltas below a configurable threshold to reduce bandwidth and surface only meaningful token confidence changes. The role-pruner maps unknown or noisy role labels (e.g. "assistant_bot", "system_user") into canonical roles (system, user, assistant) while preserving an optional role_metadata bag in streaming envelopes. The default mapping is conservative to avoid breaking customers who depend on custom roles.

API surface: new v1/chat query params (all optional, backward compatible): compact_output (boolean) - when true, server collapses repeated tool JSON fragments and returns a single 'tool_result' object per tool invocation; compact_mode (string) - "compact"|"verbose" controls how much ancillary metadata is included; stop_hint_weight (float 0-1) - higher values prefer explicit stop param over model-internal stops; logprob_filter_threshold (float 0-1) - tokens with probability below threshold will have logprobs suppressed in streaming deltas to reduce bandwidth. The existing 'function_call' behavior is unchanged unless compact_output is enabled.

Runtime changes: introduced runtime/stream/coordinator.py to manage stream fragment coalescing with backpressure awareness, integrated compacting logic into the v1 adapter to avoid double-encoding, and added a small heuristic to avoid coalescing across user-turn boundaries. Implemented a lightweight role-pruner in server/adapter/openai_compat/v1_chat_adapter.py that consults a normalization table and emits a role_hints field on the streaming envelope to surface pruned-original-role for observability.

Compatibility and safety: defaults preserve current behavior (compact_output=false, stop_hint_weight=0.5, logprob_filter_threshold=0.01). Role-pruner requires an opt-in flag (role_prune=true) for strict normalization; otherwise we only emit role_hints. The change intentionally avoids rewriting function signatures or existing response envelopes to prevent breaking existing integrations.

Testing and benchmarks: added integration tests under tests/integration/test_toolflow_streaming.py and unit tests for coordinator heuristics. Benchmarks run on perf-canary showed ~9-12% reduction in outbound streaming tokens for workloads with verbose tool JSONs (internal benchmark workload 'toolsuite-large-2025'), and ~3-6% average reduction in end-to-end latency due to fewer serialization events.

Docs and migration: updated docs/openai_compat.md with example calls, recommended migration path, and a sample shim for SDKs to opt-in to compact_output gradually. Release notes include guidance on when to enable compact_output and how to tune logprob_filter_threshold for telemetry vs fidelity.

Checklist: - [x] Runtime coordinator implementation + unit tests - [x] Role-pruner with opt-in flag - [x] SDK sample snippet and doc update - [x] Perf benchmark and canary run - [x] Security review for envelope changes

Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the compact representation as an opt-in addition. We observed a handful of legacy clients that relied on repeated fragments for incremental parsing; for those customers we recommend gradual migration with compact_output=false while testing SDK helpers that reconstruct streamed fragments into a compact object on the client side.

Summary of changes: added an adapter-level coordinator that: 1) recognizes and collapses repeated tool JSON fragments into a single compact structure when clients request compact output (new query param compact_output=true), 2) supplies a configurable stop_hint_priority so that explicit stop sequences sent by the API call are respected ahead of model-internal heuristics when desired, and 3) filters streaming logprob deltas below a configurable threshold to reduce bandwidth and surface only meaningful token confidence changes. The role-pruner maps unknown or noisy role labels (e.g. "assistant_bot", "system_user") into canonical roles (system, user, assistant) while preserving an optional role_metadata bag in streaming envelopes. The default mapping is conservative to avoid breaking customers who depend on custom roles.

API surface: new v1/chat query params (all optional, backward compatible): compact_output (boolean) - when true, server collapses repeated tool JSON fragments and returns a single 'tool_result' object per tool invocation; compact_mode (string) - "compact"|"verbose" controls how much ancillary metadata is included; stop_hint_weight (float 0-1) - higher values prefer explicit stop param over model-internal stops; logprob_filter_threshold (float 0-1) - tokens with probability below threshold will have logprobs suppressed in streaming deltas to reduce bandwidth. The existing 'function_call' behavior is unchanged unless compact_output is enabled.

Runtime changes: introduced runtime/stream/coordinator.py to manage stream fragment coalescing with backpressure awareness, integrated compacting logic into the v1 adapter to avoid double-encoding, and added a small heuristic to avoid coalescing across user-turn boundaries. Implemented a lightweight role-pruner in server/adapter/openai_compat/v1_chat_adapter.py that consults a normalization table and emits a role_hints field on the streaming envelope to surface pruned-original-role for observability.

Compatibility and safety: defaults preserve current behavior (compact_output=false, stop_hint_weight=0.5, logprob_filter_threshold=0.01). Role-pruner requires an opt-in flag (role_prune=true) for strict normalization; otherwise we only emit role_hints. The change intentionally avoids rewriting function signatures or existing response envelopes to prevent breaking existing integrations.

Testing and benchmarks: added integration tests under tests/integration/test_toolflow_streaming.py and unit tests for coordinator heuristics. Benchmarks run on perf-canary showed ~9-12% reduction in outbound streaming tokens for workloads with verbose tool JSONs (internal benchmark workload 'toolsuite-large-2025'), and ~3-6% average reduction in end-to-end latency due to fewer serialization events.

Docs and migration: updated docs/openai_compat.md with example calls, recommended migration path, and a sample shim for SDKs to opt-in to compact_output gradually. Release notes include guidance on when to enable compact_output and how to tune logprob_filter_threshold for telemetry vs fidelity.

Checklist: - [x] Runtime coordinator implementation + unit tests - [x] Role-pruner with opt-in flag - [x] SDK sample snippet and doc update - [x] Perf benchmark and canary run - [x] Security review for envelope changes

Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the compact representation as an opt-in addition. We observed a handful of legacy clients that relied on repeated fragments for incremental parsing; for those customers we recommend gradual migration with compact_output=false while testing SDK helpers that reconstruct streamed fragments into a compact object on the client side.

title: generate delta upgrade index and SBOM stamping for private-installer CI
preview: This PR adds a CI-first pipeline to produce deterministic delta upgrade packages and stamp SBOM/provenance metadata into the private-installer release artifacts. Motivation: customers running Private (VPC/on-prem) need compact upgrade bundles and a verifiable upgrade graph so operators can perform safe roll-forwards and rollbacks without re-pulling full images. The output of the pipeline is a signed delta-index (delta-index.json) that expresses available upgrade paths between installer versions and references per-artifact SBOMs, Helm chart lockfiles, and Terraform module plan snapshots. Changes include: 1) new pipeline job (private-installer:delta-pack) that builds compact tarballs containing only changed manifests and image digests; 2) delta-indexer tool (tools/release/delta-indexer.go) which computes graph edges and emits SBOM references; 3) SBOM stamping step using syft and a lightweight metadata envelope; 4) cosign/cosign-wrapper integration to attach OCI signatures and timestamped provenance to each delta bundle; 5) Helm chart lockfile generator (charts/lockfiles/) and Terraform module plan snapshotter (terraform/modules/plan-snapshots/); 6) makefile and CI matrix additions to validate deltas with a staging smoke deploy and terraform plan dry-run. Checklist: - [x] implement delta-index format and generator - [x] wire up CI job and pipeline config - [x] add SBOM generation and envelope signing - [x] add unit tests for indexer and SBOM parsing - [x] add e2e smoke test in staging workflow - [x] document format and CLI usage in docs/release/delta-index.md Backwards compatibility: existing full-installer artifacts and release flow are unchanged; the delta-index is additive and consumed by the private-installer CLI/upgrade controller. Note on security: SBOMs are generated with syft v0.83.0 and emitted alongside artifacts; cosign keys are stored in the CI KMS and rotated per release cadence. See linked Linear tickets for rollout plan and gating criteria.

title: weighted-queue-and-kv-pin-timeouts-to-mitigate-starvation
preview: Context: Production traces and perf canaries exposed recurring tail-latency regressions under mixed workloads with hot and cold tenants. Root cause analysis showed that the continuous batching scheduler's FIFO-dominant queue allowed small high-priority requests to be repeatedly overtaken when the runtime favored large batched prefixes; simultaneously the KV cache had a long pin expiry that amplified hot-key monopolization. This PR introduces two coordinated changes: 1) a lightweight weighted-queue throttler in the continuous batcher that enforces service-share weights at queue admission time (soft scheduling), and 2) adjustable KV-cache pin timeouts and a small-metadata eviction hint to reduce long-running pin monopolies. Design: - Admissions now record a per-queue weight token and a sliding-window deficit counter; long-running peers lose admission weight over configurable windows to prevent starvation. - The scheduler still batches by model/seq-length compatibility, but before combining candidates we apply a fast O(1) fairness filter that reorders a small prefix of the queue based on deficit and age. This preserves batching efficiency for the common case while bounding unfairness. - KV cache: introduce per-tenant pin_ttl (default lowered from 30s -> 8s) and a 'soft-evict' hint that allows cold-key compaction during sustained high memory pressure. - Feature gate: both features are behind runtime flags (batcher.weighted_queue=true, kv.pin_adaptive=true) and can be tuned via config map in Dedicated/Private. Implementation notes: - runtime/scheduler/continuous_batcher.rs: added WeightedQueue struct, deficit accounting, admission helpers, unit tests for corner cases. - runtime/scheduler/queue.rs: added peek-and-reorder primitive with micro-optimizations to avoid allocations. - runtime/kv_cache/pinning.rs: introduced pin_ttl, soft_evict hints, metrics for pin_count and pin_ttl_hist. - tests/scheduler_integration.rs: new deterministic multi-tenant load harness and long-tail assertion checks. - docs/ops/continuous-batcher-tuning.md: operator-facing notes and suggested knob values for common workloads. Performance summary: - Canary synthetic workload (10 tenants; mix heavy/light): P95 request latency reduced by 22% for light tenants and P99 reduced by 14% overall when feature gate enabled; end-to-end throughput unchanged within 3%. - Microbench: weighted admission adds ~150ns median overhead per admission check; observed <1% CPU cost at 100k qps. Testing and rollout: - Unit tests added for deficit accounting, corner cases for zero-weight tenants, and KV pin expiry behaviour. - Integration canary run in staging for 48h before merge. - Rollout plan: enable behind feature gate for 10% of Dedicated clusters, observe tail metrics for 72h, then ramp to 100% with monitor playbook. Backwards compatibility: defaults preserve existing behavior (feature gates off). Related work: builds on ENG-7120 (continuous-batcher refactor) and pairs with upcoming kernel-level scheduling hints (ENG-8350).

title: fp8-autotune-dequant-rewrite-and-kernel-router
preview:
Motivation: We observed cache-sensitive and sequence-length-dependent regressions when serving FP8/INT8 model variants on the high-throughput path. Two root causes surfaced in profiling: (1) redundant dequantization stubs inserted by the compiler leading to extra memory traffic across the KV cache boundary, and (2) suboptimal kernel selection for mixed FP8/INT8 attention matmuls at mid-sequence lengths, which increases both latency tail and per-token GPU utilization. This PR introduces an autotuned FP8 dequant rewrite pass (dequant-rewriter) plus a scheduler-aware kernel router that picks kernels based on runtime signals (sequence position, KV cache hit pattern, and batching profile).

Summary of changes:
- Implement dequant-rewriter: a graph-level pass that coalesces adjacent dequant ops, hoists dequantization out of hot KV cache paths when safe, and emits a fused "dequant+transpose" kernel where applicable. The pass supports conservative aliasing checks and a feature-flagged faster mode that assumes stable KV shapes in Dedicated deployments.
- FP8 autotuner: offline calibration tool and lightweight runtime probe that chooses between FP8-fused kernels, hybrid FP8/INT8 kernels, or fallback FP16 kernels per model and per-device. Tuner's decisions are persisted in model-serving metadata and can be overridden by rollout flags.
- Kernel router: scheduler integration that consumes continuous-batching statistics (batch size distribution, arrival jitter) and KV cache telemetry to choose kernel variants at request-time. Router minimizes kernel switches within a batch and prefers kernels that maintain low memory traffic across KV boundaries.
- Scheduler hints: expose a small set of hints (seq_pos_bucket, kv_hot_ratio, batch_shape_key) from the serving runtime to the kernel selector. These are lightweight scalar tags attached to batch descriptors.
- Tests and regressions: microbenchmarks for gbench and internal canary; unit tests for the dequant-rewriter; end-to-end perf test in the perf-canary workflow.
- Documentation: notes in docs/serving/quantization.md and a new troubleshooting section describing common FP8 failure modes and mitigation.

Design notes and rationale:
- We avoided aggressive whole-graph FP8 lowering because (a) some models use dynamic dispatch patterns incompatible with a single lowering, and (b) dequantization hoisting across KV cache write-back needs conservative checks to avoid stale keys. The rewriter therefore applies targeted fusion and hoisting, with a safe fallback to existing dequant ops when aliasing cannot be proven.
- Autotuner workflow: an offline pass analyzes representative prompts to build a candidate set. At runtime, a short probe (10s of warmup tokens) validates latency/throughput and writes a per-model best-variant tag. For private and dedicated tenants, the faster autotune mode is enabled by default; hosted tenants require opt-in.

Benchmarks (NVIDIA A100s, sequence mix representative of chat workloads):
- Baseline: mixed FP16 path (pre-change)
  - P50 latency: 86ms
  - P95 latency: 220ms
  - Tokens/sec: 35k
- Post-change (autotuned FP8-fused kernels + dequant-rewriter):
  - P50 latency: 72ms (16% reduction)
  - P95 latency: 165ms (25% reduction)
  - Tokens/sec: 45k (28% increase)
- For small-batch/high-jitter scenarios the kernel router avoided pathological kernel switches and reduced tail variance by ~30%.

Cost impact: in Dedicated benchmarks the improvements correspond to ~22% lower token-GPU-hours for the measured workload mix.

Rollout plan and safety:
- Feature-flagged under runtime.feature.fp8_autotune; default off for hosted mainline for 48h while we collect telemetry.
- Canary deployment to perf-canary and one Dedicated customer (internal) for 72h.
- Automatic rollback policy: if P99 latency increases by >10% or error rate increases by >0.5% over baseline within a 10m window, the rollout controller reverts to the previous kernel tag.

CI and validation:
- Unit and integration tests added under runtime/quant and serving/scheduler.
- Perf canary runs showed initial flakiness due to an aliasing edge-case; fixed by strengthening the alias analysis in the rewriter.
- CI: initial run failed one flaky test (serve_kv_integration_test) and was rerun: final CI passed.

Migration and compatibility notes:
- Models that embed custom kernels may require a one-line model metadata opt-in: "quant.auto_tune: true". We added a compatibility probe that logs recommended kernels without changing runtime behavior until opt-in.
- No API surface change to the Redwood public API.

Observability:
- New metrics: quant_kernel.variant_chosen, quant_autotune.probe_latency, quant_dequant.fused_ops.
- Traces: added tags for seq_pos_bucket and kernel_variant to GPU traces to make regressions easier to root-cause.

Related work: closes ENG-4231 (autotune baseline), progresses ENG-4278 (KV-aware scheduling).

Commits summary: see commits field below.

Files changed (high level): see changed_files field below.

Follow-ups:
- Add FP8 support to the GPU profiler export (ENG-4305).
- Expand autotune dataset to include longer-form decoding patterns.
- Evaluate similar rewriter for INT8-only models where dequantization churn is observed.

title: Dependency-aware rollout orchestrator with safety chains and cohort quarantine
preview:
Motivation: We frequently run multi-model experiments (A/B tests, shadow+canary hybrids) where some models are dependent on upstream feature transformations or other model variants. Current rollouts treat each variant independently which can lead to invalid traffic splits and unsafe rollbacks when dependent cohorts are promoted or quarantined. This PR introduces a dependency-aware rollout orchestrator that models 'safety chains' between rollout nodes so that promotion/rollback decisions are propagated deterministically across related cohorts.

Summary of changes: Adds a new orchestration component (dependency orchestrator) to the servegraph pipeline, implements safety-chain semantics, and extends the rollout policy language with two new directives: "requires" (declare dependencies) and "quarantine_on_failure" (automatic cohort isolation). The orchestrator supports: 1) transactional commit semantics for multi-cohort promotions, 2) automatic quarantine propagation (if a dependent cohort fails SLOs, its dependents are quarantined), 3) staged A/B tie-breaking rules when cohorts share traffic windows, and 4) integration points for Redwood Console to visualize dependency graphs and quarantine status.

Compatibility/migration: Existing rollout specs remain valid; new behaviors are opt-in via the 'dependency_mode: enabled' flag on rollout configs. For teams that want automatic dependency inference, we provide a best-effort heuristic that can be toggled off. No breaking API changes; CLI/SDK support for the new directives added in separate SDK PRs.

Testing: Unit coverage for orchestration logic, integration harness that runs multi-cohort simulations (synthetic latency/error distributions), and a canary e2e with a shadowed dependent model running in the perf-canary lab. A flaky quota test was retried in CI; final green run attached.

Operational notes: When dependency_mode is enabled, redeploys create a transient 'planning' phase for up to 30s where traffic is frozen while the orchestrator evaluates commit plans. This avoids partial promotions. We also emit new metrics: rollout.dependency.plan_duration, rollout.dependency.quarantine_count, and rollout.dependency.propagation_errors. Documented in the rollout runbook and console changelog.

Checklist:
- [x] design doc (linked in ENG-1782)
- [x] unit + integration tests
- [x] console visualization hooks
- [x] migration path and docs

Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the orchestration core separate from the scheduling/batching runtime to preserve low-latency paths for token serving. Follow-up PRs will add autoscale-aware ordering for promotions to minimize capacity churn.

title: openai-v1-chat-params-clarity-and-toolcall-debounce-for-stream-stability
preview:
Motivation: We continue to close the gap between Redwood's /v1/chat compatibility surface and the OpenAI chat/completions expectations. Customers reported two classes of issues: (1) noisy rapid fncall footprints during network retries and websocket reconnects that caused multiple tool invocations, and (2) ambiguous behavior when multiple stop signals, role aliases, or logprob fallbacks co-occurred in streaming output. This PR implements two coordinated changes: (A) a parameter clarity layer that normalizes and documents v1 chat params (role aliases, stop token semantics, logprob backoff) so downstream stages get a deterministic shape; (B) a lightweight toolcall debounce and stream heartbeat handshake that prevents duplicated function/tool invocations on transient reconnects and provides a clearer stream parity signal to clients. 

What changed (high level):
- Added a v1 param canonicalizer that: maps role aliases (e.g. assistant/assistant_bot -> assistant), coercively flattens stop lists into prioritized sequences, and exposes an explicit logprob fallback strategy field so the runtime can decide deterministic fallback ordering.
- Introduced a ToolCallDebouncer component (in the streaming runtime) that groups function call events within a short, configurable window and emits a single canonical tool invocation when the payloads are equivalent or supersets. Debounce window defaults to 150ms; policy is configurable per-route.
- Implemented a StreamHeartbeat handshake token inserted into the framed streaming envelope to help clients reason about stream continuity and replay semantics. This is backward-compatible: older clients will ignore the heartbeat key.
- Small changes to the OpenAI compatibility adapter so that v1/chat params are translated into a stable internal config object consumed by the streamer and tool dispatcher.

Why this design:
- Debounce (vs dedupe) preserves intent for near-simultaneous distinct calls but prevents accidental duplicate tool effect when identical payloads are retried.
- Canonical param shape reduces branching across the stack (routing, batching, evals) and simplifies rollout of future stop/logprob policies.
- Heartbeat token makes it feasible to implement at-most-once tool semantics in the face of websocket reconnects without a heavy transactional system.

Checklist: 
- [x] param canonicalizer implemented and unit-tested
- [x] toolcall debounce with config and integration tests
- [x] stream heartbeat handshake integrated into framed streaming envelope
- [x] updated docs with migration notes for customers using advanced stop/logprob behavior
- [x] load/perf smoke tests run on staging (no regression in p50 latency; slight p99 increase for streams with debounce enabled, measured and documented)

Testing and results: 
- Unit tests: src/compat/test_param_mapper.py covers role mapping and stop prioritization permutations.
- Integration: tests simulate websocket reconnects with rapid fncall retries; prior to debounce we observed 2–4 duplicate tool invocations in 12% of reconnection scenarios; after changes duplicates drop to <1%.
- Performance: enabling debounce adds ~0.8ms median overhead to streaming path when active; default off for customers unless route config enables it.

Backwards compatibility: default behavior preserves existing semantics unless a route explicitly opts into the debounce policy. The canonicalizer will not mutate user-visible strings except for role alias normalization, which is documented as a safe mapping.

Related work: ENG-1604 (stream-framing maturity), ENG-1712 (fncall reliability post-incident), SUP-4211 (customer report leading to this PR).

Commits summary: 
- initial: param canonicalizer and tests
- add: toolcall debouncer core logic + integration tests
- add: heartbeat envelope field and streamer wiring
- docs: openai_compat.md migration guidance and default config notes
- fix: handle edge-case when stop list is empty and logprob fallback is present

Changed files (high level):
- src/compat/param_mapper.py (new)
- src/streaming/heartbeat_manager.py (new)

text:
Motivation: We continue to close the gap between Redwood's /v1/chat compatibility surface and the OpenAI chat/completions expectations. Customers reported two classes of issues: (1) noisy rapid fncall footprints during network retries and websocket reconnects that caused multiple tool invocations, and (2) ambiguous behavior when multiple stop signals, role aliases, or logprob fallbacks co-occurred in streaming output. This PR implements two coordinated changes: (A) a parameter clarity layer that normalizes and documents v1 chat params (role aliases, stop token semantics, logprob backoff) so downstream stages get a deterministic shape; (B) a lightweight toolcall debounce and stream heartbeat handshake that prevents duplicated function/tool invocations on transient reconnects and provides a clearer stream parity signal to clients. 

What changed (high level):
- Added a v1 param canonicalizer that: maps role aliases (e.g. assistant/assistant_bot -> assistant), coercively flattens stop lists into prioritized sequences, and exposes an explicit logprob fallback strategy field so the runtime can decide deterministic fallback ordering.
- Introduced a ToolCallDebouncer component (in the streaming runtime) that groups function call events within a short, configurable window and emits a single canonical tool invocation when the payloads are equivalent or supersets. Debounce window defaults to 150ms; policy is configurable per-route.
- Implemented a StreamHeartbeat handshake token inserted into the framed streaming envelope to help clients reason about stream continuity and replay semantics. This is backward-compatible: older clients will ignore the heartbeat key.
- Small changes to the OpenAI compatibility adapter so that v1/chat params are translated into a stable internal config object consumed by the streamer and tool dispatcher.

Why this design:
- Debounce (vs dedupe) preserves intent for near-simultaneous distinct calls but prevents accidental duplicate tool effect when identical payloads are retried.
- Canonical param shape reduces branching across the stack (routing, batching, evals) and simplifies rollout of future stop/logprob policies.
- Heartbeat token makes it feasible to implement at-most-once tool semantics in the face of websocket reconnects without a heavy transactional system.

Checklist: 
- [x] param canonicalizer implemented and unit-tested
- [x] toolcall debounce with config and integration tests
- [x] stream heartbeat handshake integrated into framed streaming envelope
- [x] updated docs with migration notes for customers using advanced stop/logprob behavior
- [x] load/perf smoke tests run on staging (no regression in p50 latency; slight p99 increase for streams with debounce enabled, measured and documented)

Testing and results: 
- Unit tests: src/compat/test_param_mapper.py covers role mapping and stop prioritization permutations.
- Integration: tests simulate websocket reconnects with rapid fncall retries; prior to debounce we observed 2–4 duplicate tool invocations in 12% of reconnection scenarios; after changes duplicates drop to <1%.
- Performance: enabling debounce adds ~0.8ms median overhead to streaming path when active; default off for customers unless route config enables it.

Backwards compatibility: default behavior preserves existing semantics unless a route explicitly opts into the debounce policy. The canonicalizer will not mutate user-visible strings except for role alias normalization, which is documented as a safe mapping.

Related work: ENG-1604 (stream-framing maturity), ENG-1712 (fncall reliability post-incident), SUP-4211 (customer report leading to this PR).

title: Invoke compatibility proto mapper and decision telemetry
preview: Motivation: Customers expect OpenAI-style function/tool calling to 'just work' when routed through Redwood's serving runtime. However, in practice we see a set of recurring issues: heterogeneous tool descriptors from third-party models, ambiguous tool_choice semantics when multiple tools match, and stream fragmentation that splits the tool invocation payload across SSE boundaries. This PR introduces a compatibility-focused proto mapper and decision telemetry that together make tool invocation more deterministic and observable.Design summary: 1) proto_mapper: a lightweight rule engine that canonicalizes incoming OpenAI-compatible invocation envelopes into a single Redwood internal representation. It normalizes fields (name/description/schema/arguments), expands legacy aliases into canonical redwood_tool_id, and attaches a compat_hint when the mapping required heuristics. Mappings are deterministic and extensible via a small YAML ruleset shipped with the package. 2) choice_scorer: when multiple tools match a requested function name/schema, the scorer computes an ordered candidate list using schema affinity (how well the provided arguments conform to each tool schema), stream-priming context (tokens already generated that imply certain tool semantics), and operational priors (recent latency/cost observed for the customer's tenant). The winner receives an explicit confidence score and a short rationale encoded in metadata. 3) stream_coordinator: improves streaming stability by aligning fragments to logical tool frames: partial JSON fragments are buffered and resynchronized rather than forwarded to downstream tool adapters; we emit a fragment_resync event for observability. 4) telemetry: emit an invoke.decision event for every tool invocation that contains candidate list, scores, chosen_index, confidence, request_id, and quick pointers to the proto_mapper rule that fired. This event is low-cardinality and intended for debugging regressions and production investigations.Changes and files: See changed_files for a compact list. Key behavioral changes are limited and guarded behind a feature flag 'openai_compat_proto_mapper' that defaults to on for Hosted only and off for Dedicated/Private until customers opt in. Tests: added unit tests covering nested JSON arguments, missing schema resolution, alias expansion, and integration tests that simulate streaming SSE fragmentation across multiple boundaries to verify deterministic reassembly and correct candidate scoring. Performance: choice_scorer is designed to be cheap (fast schema affinity checks and cached token-based signals). Benchmarks on a 4k sequence workload show median decision latency ~= 0.9ms additional per-invoke at p50 and 2.4ms at p95 under load (detailed numbers in benchmark artifact attached to CI). Rollout and risk mitigation: rollout is staged. Hosted -> 10%/50%/100% over two weeks with monitoring dashboards. Dedicated/Private customers will see release notes and can enable via console or config. We included a migration script to generate alias mappings for large customers that rely on legacy tool names.Open questions and future work: - Consider surfacing the decision rationale in debug traces for customers with private deployments. - Expand rule engine to accept safe machine-suggested mappings (requires guardrails). - Add optional delta compression for repeated identical invocation envelopes to reduce egress cost.Review conversation snapshot: See review_thread field for representative comments. Merge strategy: squash; small logical commits squashed to a single logical change and merged to main.Approvals: Approved by core runtime reviewers and SRE, CI passed and manual integration harness validated in staging.

title: tooling/execution: concurrency safeguards and tool-result-injection ergonomics
preview: Motivation: Customers integrating complex tool chains reported flaky outputs when multiple tools ran in parallel and tool results were injected back into the model stream. This PR standardizes execution semantics for multi-tool calls, adds runtime safeguards for parallelism, and harmonizes behavior with our OpenAI-compat surface. Summary of changes: 1) Executor: introduce a per-request "tool-parallelism" cap (configurable at API/route level) that bounds concurrent tool invocations and provides backpressure hooks to the scheduler; 2) Injection protocol: formalize a deterministic injection ordering when multiple tool results arrive within the same model turn and attach an idempotency token to each injected result so retries do not duplicate content; 3) Retry semantics: new retry policy enum (none, best-effort, strict-idempotent) exposed via the API and respected by the runtime and OpenAI-compat shim; 4) Streaming stability: when streaming responses include tool-calls, we now atomically flush tool-invocation markers to the stream and gracefully resume generation once injected results are committed to the KV cache; 5) OpenAI-compat updates: function-calling payloads now accept an optional "execution_hints" map with parallelism and idempotency hints that the compat layer translates into our internal routing and executor options; 6) Observability: emit new metrics (tool.invocations.active, tool.invocations.queued, tool.injection.latency) and traces annotated with execution_hints for easier debugging. Implementation notes: - Executor changes are confined to runtime/executor/* and api/handlers/tool_call_handler.go; the executor preserves the previous scheduling algorithm but applies per-request parallelism caps before dispatching tool workers. - Injection order uses a deterministic comparator: (request_tool_sequence, tool_priority, start_timestamp). This avoids nondeterministic interleaving in model-visible outputs. - Idempotency tokens are short-lived UUIDs persisted in the request context and kept in the per-request token-cache; replayed tool results with matching token are ignored by the injection layer. - The OpenAI compatibility shim accepts additional fields but falls back to previous behavior when execution_hints are omitted to preserve backward compatibility. Checklist: - [x] Design doc updated with sequence diagrams and failure modes (ENG-1523) - [x] Unit tests for ordering and idempotency - [x] Integration tests simulating multi-tool fan-out and parallel retries - [x] Metrics/tracing added and tested in canary - [x] Backwards compatibility verified for existing function-calling clients - [x] Docs snippet for API surface added Notes on tradeoffs: We intentionally chose a conservative default parallelism cap of 4 to reduce burst load on downstream tool services; customers can raise this per-route with a warning in the console. The idempotency token approach requires minimal state but does not protect against cross-request duplicate side-effects (we call this out in the design doc). Migration: No action required from existing users; new execution_hints fields are additive. For customers wanting deterministic injection ordering across retries, they should include explicit tool priority in their tool manifest. Rollout plan: Canary on a small percentage of hosted traffic for one week with annotated traces and enhanced logging; force-enable flag included to accelerate rollouts for enterprise tenants. Backwards compatibility: tests included to ensure older SDKs (OpenAI-compat) do not break. Risk and mitigations: The biggest risk is increased latency when a tool result arrives slightly after generation — mitigated by configurable timeouts and an async-fallback policy that allows the generator to continue with a best-effort placeholder if strict-idempotent is not set. Local testing: see integration test harness in tests/integration/tool_invoke_fanout_test.go. CI: all unit/integration checks passed after two reruns (flaky test fix in metrics). Merge outcome: squash-merged to main with commit 0a9f3d2 (executor: add parallelism cap + injection protocol).

title: introduce-budget-mapper-mini-batcher-defaults-and-lightweight-quant-profiles
preview:
Motivation: many tenants operate with low to moderate QPS where full tenant-wide prefill and large batch policies add cost without improving tail latency. This PR adds a lightweight 'budget mapper' that emits per-tenant mini-batcher defaults and an opinionated set of small quantization profiles so teams can get cost-down defaults that are safe for production.

Summary of changes: adds a BudgetMapper component that infers conservative mini-batching and cache window defaults from historical unit-econ signals (p95 latency, tokens-per-request, cold-start rate). Introduces a tiny, validated quant_profiles.yaml with three compact profiles (tiny-int8, lean-int4, compat-fp16) and a CLI subcommand 'redwood econ inspect' to preview tenant defaults. Defaults are intentionally conservative and include automatic safe fallbacks (no quantization for models flagged as unsafe). The PR wires telemetry (econ.unit_price_estimate, batching.shape_hint, quant.profile_applied) and a lightweight e2e smoke test that validates latency and token-cost regressions against a golden workload.

Design notes: BudgetMapper is implemented as a single-file controller (pkg/econ/budget_mapper.go) that consumes aggregated profile snapshots (already emitted by existing telemetry) and runs a deterministic rule set to produce config suggestions. The decision graph is intentionally small so it can be audited (no ML training). Mini-batcher defaults focus on microbatch_size, per-tenant prefix_cache_seconds, and warmup_window_ms. Quant suggestion heuristics prefer lean-int4 for non-sensitive models with median request length < 256 and token-efficiency > 0.8.

Testing & safety: unit tests for decision branches, CI smoke (ci/econ-smoke.yml) against a local mock runtime, and an e2e harness that replays a sampled tenant workload to assert no >5% regression in p95 latency and at least 8% reduction in measured token cost for qualifying tenants. We also added a feature flag (feature.econ.budget_mapper.enabled) and rollout steps in the console so operators can opt-in per-tenant.

Migration / rollout: feature gated behind console flag and an opt-in CLI. Default remains off for existing tenants. Operators can run 'redwood econ inspect --tenant TID --dry-run' to preview suggested defaults without applying them. If applied, changes are stored as tenant overrides and can be rolled back with 'redwood econ rollback'.

Checklist:
- [x] BudgetMapper core implementation
- [x] quant_profiles.yaml (three profiles) and validation schema
- [x] CLI preview and apply/rollback helpers
- [x] telemetry hooks and unit-econ metrics
- [x] unit tests and e2e smoke
- [x] docs (observability/econ-telemetry.md)

Related: ENG-3124 contains the decision criteria; PM-198 is the product brief for conservative defaults. SUP-411 was consulted for tenant opt-in UX during an earlier support escalation.

title: Reduce CPU serialization overhead and introduce request-affine thread pinning
preview:
Motivation: production canaries showed CPU-side stalls in the request path under medium tail-load (concurrent short requests). Root cause analysis indicated three correlated issues: a global serialization lock in the request marshalling path, KV-cache read contention on a small number of shards, and excessive cross-thread wakeups from the executor when continuous batching is active. This PR addresses those CPU-side bottlenecks with low-risk, togglable changes that reduce serialization overhead and make scheduling request-affine so hot requests avoid context migration.

Summary of changes: I) Replace global request-serialization mutex with per-worker small-buffer fast-path using FlatBuffers for marshalling and a fallback to proto when necessary. II) Introduce a request-affine thread-pool (request-affine executors) that pins a request's lifecycle to a CPU core group to reduce cache churn during multi-stage request handling. III) Add a lock-free fast read path for KV-cache using a seqlock-style version stamp per shard plus per-shard read counters, and shard rebalancer to spread hot keys. IV) Reduce continuous-batching wakeups by batching wake signals and using a wait-free work-stealing hint for low-latency tails.

Performance impact (canary + micro-bench): - End-to-end P50: -6% latency, P95: -14%, P99: -18% for short inference (<128 tokens) workloads. - CPU sys time on sandboxed 8-vCPU nodes: -35% for the runtime process when under 500rps of small requests. - Throughput improved by ~9% in multi-tenant density tests due to reduced serialization stalls.

Rollout plan: flags added to runtime config and console: runtime.cpu_optimizations.enabled (default: false), runtime.request_affinity.policy (off|pinned|affine). Default rollout: enable on Dedicated clusters for canary group A (5% traffic) for 72 hours, compare P99 and host CPU; if stable, increase to 25% then 100%. Quick rollback: set runtime.cpu_optimizations.enabled=false -> instant drain-of-new-requests (no binary change).

Compatibility and safety: changes are behind config flags. No model artifacts changed. Unit and integration tests added. This is not a breaking API change.

Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for serialization fast-path and fallback - [x] KV-cache microbench and canary harness - [x] End-to-end canary config + dashboard widgets - [x] Documentation for new runtime flags and helm values - [x] Security review (no secrets touched)

Commits: detailed below.

Notes for reviewers: focus review on correctness of the seqlock implementation (KV-cache) and on potential live-migration interactions with request affinity. I added a conservative fairness timer on the affine pool (200ms) to avoid starvation of low-priority background tasks.

Additional context: This work was driven by ENG-9123 (investigate CPU stalls) and is intended to be low-risk stepping-stone; the next PR will expose adaptive shard sizing and per-tenant affinity policies.
