title: introduce-lightweight-local-dev-runner-and-toolchain-stub
preview:
Context and motivation: onboarding and fast feedback for local CI iterations has regressed as our CI matrix grew. This PR introduces a lightweight local dev runner (tools/fastdev) and a minimal toolchain stub (dev/Toolchain.toml) plus a small workflow that developers can run locally or in ephemeral self-hosted runners to get the same sanity checks as CI without the full matrix. This is an opt-in developer convenience feature that reduces time-to-first-green and iteration time on lint/test changes. Summary of changes: - add tools/fastdev/runner.go: small Go binary that runs a subset of checks in-process with conservative timeouts and filesystem caching - add tools/fastdev/README.md: usage, examples, and how to wire into VS Code tasks - add dev/Toolchain.toml: lightweight list of pinned tool versions for local dev (go, node, python, prettier, husky minimal config) - add .github/workflows/dev-local.yaml: workflow targeted at self-hosted/dev-runner label and usable as reference for local runs - add .pre-commit-config.yaml entries and Makefile target fastdev - add scripts/ci/sanity-check.sh to share common checks between CI and fastdev Why not full devcontainer? Full devcontainers are heavier and we want a quick path for contributors who already have the environment. The Toolchain.toml is intentionally minimal and human-editable; it is not authoritative for production. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (ENG-4172) [x] unit tests for runner logic [x] small e2e smoke used locally by author [x] docs and README for opt-in usage Implementation notes: the runner currently shells out to the language toolchain where appropriate but uses a caching layer (tools/fastdev/.cache) to avoid repeated installs during rapid iterations. It ships a dry-run mode and a --ci-compat flag which performs the same commands as the CI sanity lane (lint, go vet, pytest -k sanity). Small illustrative snippet of the workflow (from .github/workflows/dev-local.yaml):
name: Dev local referenced
on: [workflow_dispatch]
jobs:
  fastdev:
    runs-on: self-hosted
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: ./tools/fastdev/runner --ci-compat

text:
Context and motivation: onboarding and fast feedback for local CI iterations has regressed as our CI matrix grew. This PR introduces a lightweight local dev runner (tools/fastdev) and a minimal toolchain stub (dev/Toolchain.toml) plus a small workflow that developers can run locally or in ephemeral self-hosted runners to get the same sanity checks as CI without the full matrix. This is an opt-in developer convenience feature that reduces time-to-first-green and iteration time on lint/test changes. Summary of changes: - add tools/fastdev/runner.go: small Go binary that runs a subset of checks in-process with conservative timeouts and filesystem caching - add tools/fastdev/README.md: usage, examples, and how to wire into VS Code tasks - add dev/Toolchain.toml: lightweight list of pinned tool versions for local dev (go, node, python, prettier, husky minimal config) - add .github/workflows/dev-local.yaml: workflow targeted at self-hosted/dev-runner label and usable as reference for local runs - add .pre-commit-config.yaml entries and Makefile target fastdev - add scripts/ci/sanity-check.sh to share common checks between CI and fastdev Why not full devcontainer? Full devcontainers are heavier and we want a quick path for contributors who already have the environment. The Toolchain.toml is intentionally minimal and human-editable; it is not authoritative for production. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (ENG-4172) [x] unit tests for runner logic [x] small e2e smoke used locally by author [x] docs and README for opt-in usage Implementation notes: the runner currently shells out to the language toolchain where appropriate but uses a caching layer (tools/fastdev/.cache) to avoid repeated installs during rapid iterations. It ships a dry-run mode and a --ci-compat flag which performs the same commands as the CI sanity lane (lint, go vet, pytest -k sanity). Small illustrative snippet of the workflow (from .github/workflows/dev-local.yaml):
name: Dev local referenced
on: [workflow_dispatch]
jobs:
  fastdev:
    runs-on: self-hosted
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: ./tools/fastdev/runner --ci-compat

Changes are conservative: no production CI lane behaviors removed; existing CI still the source of truth. Rollout plan: merge to main and advertise opt-in in the contributor guide; monitor adoption and iterate. Performance expectations: local fastdev runs should be ~30-60s for small changes on modern dev machines, substantially faster than full CI matrix. Risks and mitigations: - risk: divergence between fastdev and CI -> mitigation: include --ci-compat mode that runs identical commands, and we add a CI check that runs the fastdev dry-run in a nightly job to detect drift. - risk: contributors rely on the stub as authoritative -> mitigation: call out in README that Toolchain.toml is a local convenience. Related PRs & notes: this PR intentionally avoids changing release automation and focuses on developer feedback loops; follow-ups will propose integrating a thin prebuild cache and a remote runner for fast-path checks.

title: add kernel-hotspot tracer, kvcache offset histogram, and continuous batch pressure probe
preview: Motivation: It has been difficult to correlate kernel-level hotspots with KV cache layout and continuous-batching backpressure events in production traces. This PR introduces three complementary low-overhead probes and a small microbench harness to make cause-and-effect visible during perf investigations and automated regressions. Summary of changes: (1) kernel-hotspot-tracer: lightweight sampling probe that annotates scheduler and CUDA kernel entry/exit with a compact hotspot identifier derived from callsite and op-type. Emits NVTX ranges when available and falls back to short tracepoints for environments without NVTX. Includes FLOPS-adjusted counters to avoid over-weighting long-running kernels. (2) kvcache-offset-histogram: on-KVcache-access histogram recording offset distribution and small-window reuse counts to detect pathological stride patterns introduced by certain tokenizers or model layouts. Buckets are power-of-two with a dense region for accesses under 1KB. Exported as Prometheus-friendly counters and optional trace annotations for correlation. (3) continuous-batch-pressure-probe: emits an instantaneous batch_pressure gauge when the batch queue begins to back up and a sampled per-window pressure histogram for debug builds. Hooks into the batching controller earlier in the pipeline to capture producer-side pressure and includes a token-rate estimator to reduce noise from transient spikes. (4) simple_seq_bench microbench: executable to exercise variable-length sequences, stridey KV access patterns, and kernel-heavy ops; emits a compact trace and histogram CSV for offline analysis and dashboard ingestion. Design: probes are guarded by feature flags and sample rate knobs with defaults off in release builds. Histogram storage is ring-buffer backed and uses per-worker aggregators to minimize atomics. NVTX ranges are used when available with a fallback so traces are consistent across environments. Rollout: feature-gated behind telemetry.feature_flags.kernel_hotspot and telemetry.feature_flags.kvcache_hist, default disabled. Canary rollout plan: 5 percent traffic for one week then expand if metrics are stable. Tests: unit tests for histogram bucketing and ring buffer safety, stress test for multi-producer writes, and a short GPU integration job that runs the microbench in CI. Backwards compatibility: non-breaking and opt-in. Follow-ups: small observability ingestion parser update (observability-pack) and a future probe for kernel memory-bandwidth when cupi integration is feasible.

title: per-channel requantization + kernel fusion with KV-cache hinting to improve INT8/FP8 steady-state throughput
preview:
Title: Per-channel requantization + kernel fusion with KV-cache hinting to improve INT8/FP8 steady-state throughput

Summary / Motivation:
We observed that for real traffic profiles (small interactive requests with hot prefixes) the current INT8 execution path suffers from uneven per-head/per-channel scaling which results in lower GEMM utilization and extra dequant/quant roundtrips around attention and MLP. This PR introduces a per-channel requantization stage inside the microkernel and fuses the dequant/scale operations into the attention + MLP kernels when safe. Additionally, it adds a lightweight KV-cache hint that allows the scheduler to prefer pre-warmed KV workers when requantization is detected, reducing cold-KV stalls for hot prefixes. The goal is to increase steady-state throughput without impacting worst-case correctness or model quality.

What changed (high level):
- Introduced a per-channel requantization pass in the inference runtime that produces per-channel scale/zero offsets used by fused kernels. This avoids re-quantizing to a single global scale across wide layers.
- Fused dequant + GEMM + requant hot-paths in the GPU microkernel for attention and first MLP matmuls; fallbacks exist to existing dequant/separate-gemm path when fusion preconditions fail (e.g., unsupported layout, odd channel count).
- Added a scheduler hint (kv_cache_hint) propagated from request metadata when prefix reuse is detected; scheduler uses hint to bias placement to nodes with warm KV caches. This is advisory only and respects capacity/policy boundaries.
- FP8 compatibility: added a transparent FP8 fallback where per-channel requantization indicates numeric instability from INT8 scaling; runtime will automatically switch to FP8 kernels for that layer and emit a metric.
- Extended kernel capability negotiation so the compilation pipeline can choose fused kernels at deploy time based on model/layout characteristics.
- New microbenchmarks and regression tests + updated continuous-batching integration tests to cover fused paths.

Why this is different from prior quant work:
Previous PRs optimized global/tensor-level requantization and scheduling around KV batching. This change targets per-channel variability and moves the fusion directly into microkernels to reduce memory traffic and kernel launches. The KV hint is a low-risk way to improve locality without changing core placement policies.

Benchmarks (representative):
Environment: 1xA100-80GB, Llama-2-13B quantized to INT8 using Redwood quant pipeline, synthetic traffic replay derived from production traces (mix of short interactive and streaming requests).
- Throughput (tokens/sec): +1.65x compared to baseline INT8 path at 1k token steady-state (microbatching enabled).
- Tail latency (P95 tokens/seq, 512 context): -22% (improvement).
- Token cost estimate: -11.8% due to fewer kernel launches and better utilization.
- FP8 fallback path triggered on ~2.4% of layers in stress tests; when triggered, throughput impact limited to -8% for that request while preserving quality.

Checklist / Testing:
- [x] Unit tests for per-channel scale extraction and validation
- [x] GPU microkernel unit tests (correctness vs reference FP32 for 64 random seeds)
- [x] Integration: continuous-batching + kv-cache hint end-to-end test
- [x] Perf: added benchmark harnesses and CI perf job (used to validate +1.65x)
- [x] Model quality smoke: token-level logprob deltas across evaluation prompt-set (no regression beyond tolerances)
- [x] Safety: fallback to non-fused code and FP8 if numerical checks fail at runtime

Commits (high-level):
- a3d9f1e add per-channel scale extractor and validation logic
- b7c2d1a implement fused dequant-gemm-requant microkernel (CUDA)
- c48f0d2 extend kernel registry and capability negotiation in compile pipeline
- d90b3aa add kv_cache_hint propagation and scheduler hooks
- e1f4b55 add perf harness + benchmarks for fused path
- f0a7b23 tests: unit/integration updates and flaky test fix

Files changed (illustrative, not exhaustive):
- runtime/kernels/fused_gemm_requant.cu (new)
- runtime/quant/per_channel_scale.cc
- runtime/quant/per_channel_scale.h
- runtime/compile/kernel_registry.cc
- runtime/scheduler/kv_hint_router.cc
- runtime/scheduler/kv_hint_router_test.cc
- runtime/tests/quant_fusion_integration_test.py
- bench/perf/fused-path-bench.cc
- third_party/benchmarks/trace_replay_profiles/prod_short_prefix.json (updated trace sample)

Review conversation (high level):
- Priya Natarajan (review): "Nice work — fusion looks clean. Two asks: (1) ensure we have clear perf counters for how often FP8 fallback fires, and (2) add a guard that prevents fusion on tiny channels (channel<8)."
- Author (Ethan): "Added runtime counter `quant.fallback.fp8.count` and a channel-size guard in the kernel registry; pushed patch f0a7b23."
- Glen Ortiz (review): "Scheduler hint looks reasonable but I worry about policy escape when hints are overused. Please make hint decay configurable and add metrics for hint hit-rate."
- Author: "Added `kv_hint.decay_window_ms` config with default 60s and `kv_hint.hit_rate` metric; included in release notes."
- Marta Rios (approver): "Verified bench harness and model quality smoke locally; approved pending CI."

CI summary and flakiness notes:
- Unit: pass
- Integration: initially failed due to flaky tensor-check in microkernel test; fixed by aligning epsilon and test seeds in f0a7b23; re-ran and passed.
- Perf CI: pass on dedicated perf runners; relative performance validated against a pinned baseline commit.

Compatibility and fallback behavior:
- Fusion is opt-in per-deployment via a compile-time flag and runtime capability negotiation. Default hosted deploys will enable fusion for models that pass validation; Dedicated/Private customers can opt out via config.
- In case fusion preconditions fail or numerical checks exceed thresholds, runtime falls back to existing dequant -> GEMM -> requant path and emits metrics. FP8 fallback is automatic and logged.

Operational considerations:
- New metrics: quant.fallback.fp8.count, quant.fused.kernel.calls, quant.fused.kernel.latency_ms, kv_hint.hit_rate
- Rollout plan: canary on 5% of traffic, monitor token throughput, p95 latency, and quant.fallback.fp8.count; ramp to 25% then 100% if stable.

Related linear/Jira: ENG-2045 (per-channel quant), ENG-2122 (kv-affinity hints), INT-712 (customer report re: tail spikes)

Merge outcome:
- Merged via squash on 2026-02-20 after approvals and CI green.

Notes for follow-ups / TODOs:
- Explore automatic tuning of per-channel scale binning to reduce FP8 triggers
- Investigate kernel tiling variants for odd channel counts (small-channel fallback currently more conservative)
- Add a lightweight runtime config that exposes a "fusion aggressiveness" knob to SRE teams

If you want to reproduce perf results, see bench/perf/README.md in this branch which contains exact runner flags, model artifact hashes, and trace replay params.

title: Cost Model API: add policy guardrails for what-if projections (bounds + allowed ranges)
preview: Context: The new Cost Model what-if endpoint is intentionally flexible (batching/caching/quantization knobs) and will be called by Optimize (interactive) and potentially Console (hot paths). In early staging tests and after SUP-31877 triage, we saw two failure modes: (1) callers passing extreme parameter values (e.g., batch size 4096, cache warm-rate > 1.0) leading to unrealistic savings outputs, and (2) expensive computations when callers submit high-cardinality workload summaries (large histograms / too many route keys) that can blow our latency budget and increase tail p95s. This PR adds explicit policy guardrails at the API boundary so the service is safe-by-default, deterministic, and bounded. Summary of changes: (A) Introduce a centralized PolicyGuardrails layer for what-if requests that validates and clamps input ranges (where clamping is explicitly allowed) and rejects invalid/unsafe requests with clear error codes. (B) Enforce request bounds for cardinality: max number of workload segments, max histogram buckets, max distinct route/model keys, and max scenario count per request. (C) Add a compute budget heuristic: estimate worst-case complexity from the request and reject if above threshold (protects CPU and avoids unbounded fanout to model catalog/telemetry lookups). (D) Add an allowlist for parameterized what-if dimensions in v1 (batching, prefix-cache, kv-cache, quant profile change) and explicitly reject unknown/experimental dimensions unless the caller is in an internal allowlist (feature flag + service-to-service identity). (E) Standardize error responses for guardrail failures: HTTP 400 with error_code=CM_GUARDRAIL_* for caller-correctable issues; HTTP 403 for forbidden dimensions/tenancy; HTTP 429 for rate-limit triggers; and a consistent message + remediation hints so Optimize can surface actionable UI text. Guardrails (v1): - scenario_count: max 10 scenarios per request; - workload_segments: max 25 segments (Optimize currently uses <= 8); - histogram buckets: max 32 buckets for seq_len distribution and max 32 for output_len distribution; - route/model keys: max 50 distinct keys; - batch_size_target: [1, 256] (reject outside); - microbatch_size_target: [1, 64]; - cache_hit_rate: [0.0, 1.0] (reject outside; no clamping); - cache_warm_rate: [0.0, 1.0] (reject outside; note: renamed internally to warm_rate to avoid ambiguity); - quantization_profile_id: must exist in model catalog and must be compatible with base model; - overhead_allocation_policy: must be one of {request_attributable_only, blended_cluster_overhead}; - max_estimated_compute_units: 1.0 (internal scale); reject above threshold. Observability: adds metrics cost_model.whatif.guardrail_reject_total{reason=...} and cost_model.whatif.request_shape{segments=...,buckets=...,scenarios=...} to support tuning. Also adds structured log fields guardrail_reason and request_shape_summary for incident triage. Tests: unit tests for validation + error mapping; golden tests ensuring deterministic output and stable error codes; load-test fixture ensuring guardrails prevent worst-case request shapes from entering compute. Notes: This PR intentionally rejects requests rather than silently clamping in most cases, to avoid producing misleading ROI estimates. The only clamping performed is for missing optional fields that have safe defaults (e.g., absent cache_hit_rate defaults to observed baseline). Follow-ups: (1) coordinate with Optimize to align UI input widgets with these ranges and surface error_code messages; (2) document guardrails in internal docs; (3) revisit limits after we collect two weeks of production telemetry on request shapes and latency.

title: Unify OpenAI Responses v8: seed routing + context-tag metadata passthrough
preview:
Summary: Add a compatibility layer for the incoming Responses v8 variant that preserves deterministic hints (seed/rng flags), forwards client metadata tags intact, and stabilizes streaming chunk boundaries for tool/function call reassembly. This PR centralizes the mapping logic so that other adapters (SDKs, private installer) can reuse the same translation rules. Motivation: Customers are adopting the new Responses v8 semantics (explicit seed + context-tag fields). Without a consistent mapping we see reproducibility regressions and toolcall reassembly bugs in streaming. This change reduces conversion drift and eliminates a class of flakiness observed in production canaries.

What this changes (high-level):
- Add responses-v8 adapter in pkg/openai/compat that maps v8 flags to internal RNG hints (seed-channel + deterministic-level).
- Preserve top-level client metadata (user_metadata, context_tags, annotations) through the bridge so downstream services (audit, metrics, autoroller) see the original attributes.
- Stabilize stream framing: introduce a framing anchor that pins toolcall boundary metadata on chunk edges to ensure assembled tool/function calls are not split incorrectly during resumptions or partial reconnects.
- Transparent fallbacks: if the client supplies both legacy "seed" and the new "determinism" object, prefer determinism but log the divergence for telemetry.
- Tests: unit, integration, and a new lightweight end-to-end canary to validate reassembly across connection restarts.

Design details: the compatibility layer lives under pkg/openai/compat/responses_v8_bridge.go and exposes a single function TranslateResponsesV8(reqBytes) -> InternalRequest. Key translation rules: map v8.seed -> rng_hint.seed (string), v8.determinism.level -> rng_hint.level (enum: none|soft|hard), context_tags -> request.metadata.context_tags (preserve order), user_metadata -> request.metadata.user_metadata, and structured_output.schema -> enforcement hints on the generator pipeline. For streaming, the assembler will now attach an "anchor-id" to the last chunk of a logical frame, and the reassembler reconciles chunks using anchor-id + sequence numbers to avoid mis-association when a client reconnects mid-stream.

Checklist:

Summary: Add a compatibility layer for the incoming Responses v8 variant that preserves deterministic hints (seed/rng flags), forwards client metadata tags intact, and stabilizes streaming chunk boundaries for tool/function call reassembly. This PR centralizes the mapping logic so that other adapters (SDKs, private installer) can reuse the same translation rules. Motivation: Customers are adopting the new Responses v8 semantics (explicit seed + context-tag fields). Without a consistent mapping we see reproducibility regressions and toolcall reassembly bugs in streaming. This change reduces conversion drift and eliminates a class of flakiness observed in production canaries.

What this changes (high-level):
- Add responses-v8 adapter in pkg/openai/compat that maps v8 flags to internal RNG hints (seed-channel + deterministic-level).
- Preserve top-level client metadata (user_metadata, context_tags, annotations) through the bridge so downstream services (audit, metrics, autoroller) see the original attributes.
- Stabilize stream framing: introduce a framing anchor that pins toolcall boundary metadata on chunk edges to ensure assembled tool/function calls are not split incorrectly during resumptions or partial reconnects.
- Transparent fallbacks: if the client supplies both legacy "seed" and the new "determinism" object, prefer determinism but log the divergence for telemetry.
- Tests: unit, integration, and a new lightweight end-to-end canary to validate reassembly across connection restarts.

Design details: the compatibility layer lives under pkg/openai/compat/responses_v8_bridge.go and exposes a single function TranslateResponsesV8(reqBytes) -> InternalRequest. Key translation rules: map v8.seed -> rng_hint.seed (string), v8.determinism.level -> rng_hint.level (enum: none|soft|hard), context_tags -> request.metadata.context_tags (preserve order), user_metadata -> request.metadata.user_metadata, and structured_output.schema -> enforcement hints on the generator pipeline. For streaming, the assembler will now attach an "anchor-id" to the last chunk of a logical frame, and the reassembler reconciles chunks using anchor-id + sequence numbers to avoid mis-association when a client reconnects mid-stream.

title: Introduce invocation affinity matrix and proactive alert resolver
preview:
Motivation: We observe recurring noisy SLO alerts created by correlated bursts that cross tenant thresholds without reflecting distinct root causes. Current per-invocation alerts fire independently and cause paging fatigue. This PR adds a first-class invocation affinity metric and a proactive alert resolver that groups related invocations into cohorts and suppresses/attenuates alerts when an upstream affinity signal indicates a single shared cause.

What this PR does: Adds a streaming affinity index computed at aggregation time (invocation_affinity{route, model, tenant, window}) derived from co-occurrence of invocation fingerprints, kernel contention signals, and token-phase overlap. Adds a Grafana panel to visualize the affinity matrix and an alert resolver that consumes the affinity index to decide alert coalescing and attenuation thresholds. The resolver is opt-in and governed by per-route policies (console + config).

Design notes: The affinity index is an approximate, bounded sketch (count-min + LRU eviction) to limit cardinality. Affinity score = weighted sum( fingerprint_cooccurrence, kernel_backpressure_correlation, kv_cache_overlap, token_phase_alignment ). We expose both raw and normalized scores to aid dashboarding and tuning. The proactive resolver applies a configurable hysteresis window and supports three actions: suppress, attenuate (raise threshold), and group (single alert with multi-invocation details).

Telemetry/metrics added: - invocation_affinity_score (histogram) - invocation_affinity_cohort_size (gauge) - resolver_action_count (counter) - resolver_decision_latency_ms (summary) - cohort_correlation_errors (counter)

Dashboards: New panel under Observability / Invocation cohorts: affinity matrix heatmap, cohort size timeseries, resolver action rate sparkline, example invocation bundle drilldown. Panel includes quick-links to related traces and log samples with pre-populated filters.

Alerting: Introduces an experimental alert rule template that leverages the resolver to reduce duplicate alerts across highly-affined invocations. Default behavior is off; rollout is gated behind a feature flag and gradual per-tenant opt-in.

Rollout and safety: - Feature gated by feature_flag:observability.invocation_affinity - Default config: resolver off for free-tier tenants; gradual enable for enterprise tenants with 2-week canary. - Fallback: resolver is stateless; if it fails, rules fall back to existing per-invocation alerts. All resolver decisions are logged to an audit metric for post-rollout analysis.

Testing: Unit tests for affinity sketch behavior, e2e tests that simulate correlated bursts, integration tests for dashboard links. Added a synthetic replay job that replays historical bursts to validate resolver attenuation without impacting production.

Performance: The affinity sketch uses sharded in-memory buckets with a soft size cap; measured median extra CPU per serving shard ~0.8%, 95p ~3.1% under heavy burst workloads. Token latency overhead measured <0.5ms tail in internal bench runs.

Security and privacy: Affinity calculations are performed on hashed invocation fingerprints; no raw prompt content is persisted. Access to cohort drilldown requires the existing tracing/dashboard RBAC scope.

Migration: No DB migrations. Config schema adds optional resolver policies in existing observability config.

Next steps: - Monitor resolver_action_count and cohort_correlation_errors for the first canary tenants - Iterate on dashboard UX based on SE feedback - Add an operator CLI to bulk-opt-in tenants once safe.
